a cruel, wild, uncivilised, uninhibited or brutal person

beest.e: From Middle Dutch beeste, from beste, from Old French beste, from Latin bēstia.

bouwen.e: From Middle Dutch bouwen, from Old Dutch buwan, from Proto-West Germanic *būan, from Proto-Germanic *būaną.

bedekken.e: From Middle Dutch bedecken, from Old Dutch bithekken, from Proto-West Germanic *biþakkjan, equivalent to be- + dekken. Cognate with English bethatch and English bedeck, German bedecken, Swedish betäcka.

s.def: to decide, to make a definitive choice (between options)

beslissen.e: From Middle Dutch beslissen. Equivalent to be- + slissen.

betalen.e: From Middle Dutch betalen. Equivalent to be- + talen.

examples: Blijf hier, ik ga kijken.

s.def: to stay, to remain, to still be
to keep/keep on, to continue (remain doing/behaving)
examples: Wat er ook gebeurt, altijd blijven lachen!

blijven.e: From Middle Dutch bliven, from Old Dutch blīvan (“to remain”), from Proto-West Germanic *bilīban, from Proto-Germanic *bilībaną (“to remain”).

s.def: to burn, to set aflame, to be alight
to burn, to write to a storage medium

branden.e: From Middle Dutch branden, a backformation from the past forms brande, gebrant of barnen, bernen, from Old Dutch *bernan, metathesis of brennan, from Proto-West Germanic *brannijan, from Proto-Germanic *brannijaną. Displaced the form barnen. The sense “to write to a storage medium” is a semantic ...

breken.e: From Middle Dutch brēken, from Old Dutch *brecan, from Proto-West Germanic *brekan, from Proto-Germanic *brekaną, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰreg-.

brengen.e: From Middle Dutch brengen, from Old Dutch bringan, from Proto-West Germanic *bringan, from Proto-Germanic *bringaną, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰrenk-.

s.def: to move, to be in motion
examples: Mijn benen bewegen.
Slakken bewegen zich maar langzaam.

s.def: to move, to cause to be in motion
examples: Ik kan mijn benen niet bewegen.

s.def: to budge, to motivate, to spur, to induce
examples: Ik kan hem maar niet bewegen om boodschappen te doen.

bewegen.e: From Middle Dutch bewegen. Equivalent to be- + wegen.

examples: Waarom heb je me gebeten?
Blaffende honden bijten niet.

bijten.e: From Middle Dutch biten, from Old Dutch *bītan, from Proto-West Germanic *bītan, from Proto-Germanic *bītaną, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰéydt (“to split”).

examples: Het bevatte drie doosjes en een steen.
Dat kon ik niet bevatten.

bevatten.e: From earlier bevaten, from Middle Dutch bevaten. Equivalent to be- + vatten.

s.def: to understand (concepts, ideas), to comprehend, to grasp

begrijpen.e: From Middle Dutch begripen, from Old Dutch bigrīpan, from Proto-West Germanic *bigrīpan. Equivalent to be- + grijpen.

s.def: shower (rain), or generally a bout (of bad weather such as snow)

bui.e: Of unknown origin, with no known cognates in Germanic. The word may be related to Ancient Greek φῦσα (phûsa, “blast of wind, breath”); see that entry for cognates.

examples: Mijn beroep is leraar.
Ze volgde haar passie en maakte er haar beroep van.

s.def: appeal (act of invoking or appealing to something or someone, notably a higher jurisdiction)
examples: Hij deed een beroep op de rechter voor rechtvaardigheid.
Ze deden een emotioneel beroep op het publiek om te doneren.
Hij tekende beroep aan tegen de uitspraak van de rechtbank.
Het beroep zal worden gehoord door een hogere rechtbank.

s.def: an invitation to ministry in a particular parish or congregation

beroep.e: From Middle Dutch beroep. Equivalent to a deverbal from beroepen.

examples: Er zijn boze geesten in deze bossen.

boos.e: From Middle Dutch bôse, from Old Dutch bōsi, from Proto-West Germanic *bausī, from Proto-Germanic *bausuz.

blauw.e: From Middle Dutch blau, blauwe, from Old Dutch *blāo, from Proto-West Germanic *blāu, from Proto-Germanic *blēwaz. Doublet of bleu.

s.def: happy; momentarily. For generally in life, see gelukkig.
examples: Als de straten wit zijn, zijn de kinderen blij.
Ik ben blij dat je er bent.

blij.e: With regular loss of intervocalic -d- from earlier blijde, from Middle Dutch blide, from Old Dutch *blīthi, from Proto-West Germanic *blīþī, from Proto-Germanic *blīþiz.

bewolkt.e: Ostensibly the past participle of rare and long-obsolete bewolken, but synchronically to be interpreted as pseudo-participial wolk + be- -d.

a hamlet and former municipality of Gouda, South Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Brummen, Gelderland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

broek.e: From Middle Dutch broec, from Old Dutch *bruoc, from Proto-West Germanic *brōk.

examples: In mijn cultuur is het de gewoonte dat gasten geld meenemen naar de bruiloft.

bruiloft.e: From Middle Dutch bruloft, brulocht. The first element is bruid; -loft is a derivation from the root of lopen (“to walk”) with a dental suffix (compare rucht). The original meaning would thus have been "bride-walk" or perhaps "bride-dance", "bride-leap" or even "bride-abduction" (see *hlaupaną an...

s.def: threat, menace (something that poses a risk)
threat, menace (that which expresses a threat)

boete.e: From Middle Dutch boete, from Old Dutch *bōta, from Proto-West Germanic *bōtu, from Proto-Germanic *bōtō (“recompense”). Cognate to English bote, boot (“profit, plunder”).

bijzonder.e: From earlier bezonder, from Middle Dutch besonder. Equivalent to bij (“nearby”) + zonder (“without”).

behalve.e: From Middle Dutch behalven, from Old Dutch bīhalvon (literally “at the side”), thus originally “left aside, not counted” (compare English besides).

beschermen.e: From Middle Dutch beschermen, from Old Dutch biskirmen, from Proto-West Germanic *biskirmijan. Equivalent to be- + schermen. Cognate with German beschirmen.

examples: Het vogelnestje zat hoog boven in de boom.
De kinderen speelden boven in hun slaapkamer.
De vergaderzaal bevindt zich boven, op de tweede verdieping.
Hang de foto's op de muur, net boven de bank.
Het vliegtuig vloog hoog boven de wolken.

boven.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch bōven, from Old Dutch *biovan, from Proto-West Germanic *biobanā, from *bi- + *obanā.

bijna.e: From Middle Dutch bina. Equivalent to a univerbation of bij + na. Compare German beinahe.

s.def: beaker, cup, chalice (drinking vessel, often but not always without a handle, generally not made of glass)

beker.e: From Middle Dutch beker, from Latin bicarium, probably a diminutive of Ancient Greek βῖκος (bîkos, “amphora”). Cognate with English beaker.

examples: Ik ga vanavond bij jou op bezoek.
We hebben bezoek.

bezoek.e: From Middle Dutch besoec. Equivalent to a deverbal from bezoeken.

examples: De bladeren bliezen in de wind.
Hij blaast zeepbellen.

blazen.e: From Middle Dutch blâsen, from Old Dutch *blāsan, from Proto-West Germanic *blāsan, from Proto-Germanic *blēsaną.

s.def: to ring, like using a bell
to call, by bell (originally) or (now mostly) telephone; to dial
(transitive) to bark, scold, insult, rage at

bellen.e: From Middle Dutch bellen. Equivalent to bel + -en.

examples: Het is mooi weer, laten we buiten spelen.

s.def: a mansion and its surrounding estate in the countryside

buiten.e: From Middle Dutch buten, from Old Dutch *būtan, from Proto-West Germanic *biūtan (“outside of, around, without, except”), equivalent to be- + uit + -en. Cognate to English but, Luxembourgish baussen, West Frisian bûten, Saterland Frisian buute (“without”), Dutch Low Saxon buten.

examples: Als het regent kun je beter binnen blijven.
Het schip voer de haven binnen.
U mag enkel binnen de parkeervakken parkeren.
binnen tien minuten

binnen.e: From Middle Dutch binnen, from Old Dutch *binnan, equivalent to be- + in + -en.

examples: mensen beneden de zestig

beneden.e: From Middle Dutch beneden, from Proto-Germanic *bi- + *niþanē (“from beneath”).

s.def: not sick anymore; recovered (from a disease)
examples: Word snel weer beter!
De nieuwe editie is te koop in de betere boekenwinkel.

beter.e:
From Middle Dutch beter, from Old Dutch betiro, from Proto-West Germanic *batiʀō, from Proto-Germanic *batizô, comparative of *gōdaz.
Cognate with Low German beter, West Frisian better, English better, German besser, Danish bedre, Swedish bättre.

beide.e: From Middle Dutch beide, from Old Dutch beithe.

examples: witte boorden: the white-collar class

a bit of, a little bit of, some (before mass nouns)

brazilië.e: From Portuguese Brasil + -ië, with the suffix likely under the influence of German Brasilien.

brugge.e: From Middle Dutch Brugge, from Old Dutch Bruges, Bruggas, Brugias, probably from brugga (“bridge”, now brug), ultimately from Proto-Germanic *brugjǭ.

Bosnia (a country in southeast Europe; in full, Bosnië en Herzegovina)

examples: Laten we de plannen voor het evenement bespreken tijdens de vergadering.

bespreken.e: From Middle Dutch besprēken, from Old Dutch *bisprekan, from Proto-Germanic *bisprekaną. Equivalent to be- + spreken.

examples: De binnenkant van de doos.
aan de binnenkant

banaan.e: Borrowed from Portuguese banana, probably from a West African language.

boter.e:
From Middle Dutch bōter, from Old Dutch *butera, from Proto-West Germanic *buterā, from Medieval Latin būtȳrum, from Ancient Greek βούτυρον (boúturon).
Compare Limburgish bótter, West Flemish beuter, Zealandic beuter, Middle Low German botter, German Low German Botter, Plautdietsch Botta, Old Fri...

bril.e: From Middle Dutch beril (“beryl”), from Latin beryllus (“beryl”), from Ancient Greek βήρυλλος (bḗrullos), from Prakrit [Term?] (compare Sanskrit वैडूर्य (vaiḍūrya, “a cat's eye gem; a jewel”)), from Dravidian. Probably named after the city Velur (modern day Belur) in southern India. The first gla...

s.def: beard (mass of facial hair that includes chin hair)
a village in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

baard.e: From Middle Dutch baert, from Old Dutch *bart, from Proto-West Germanic *bard, from Proto-Germanic *bardaz, Proto-Indo-European *bʰardʰ-eh₂.

boterham.e: Alteration of earlier boteram, boterram, of uncertain origin. Possibly from boter (“butter”) + *ramme, *remme (“thick slice of bread”) (compare obsolete rammel, remmel (“thick slice of bread”) and West Flemish rammeke (“toasted sandwich”)). Alternatively perhaps from boter (“butter”) + *ham (“chu...

bakken.e: From Middle Dutch backen, baken, from Old Dutch *bakkan, *bakan, from Proto-West Germanic *bakan, from Proto-Germanic *bakaną, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰeh₃g-.

s.def: to hang things on, to behang

behangen.e: From Middle Dutch behangen. Equivalent to be- + hangen.

braden.e: From Middle Dutch brâden, from Old Dutch brādan, from Proto-Germanic *brēdaną.

a permit or licence allowing one to use certain vehicles or engage in certain activities, now especially in relation to aircraft, diving, swimming (in Belgium) and sometimes mopeds

examples: Allé sjoeke, geeft me na is een bezeke, ie oep m'n kaak!
'nen Iejlen hoop beze gekocht gistre veur mene neef, veur de PC t'emme gemaokt!

blokkade.e: Borrowed from German Blockade or English blockade.

boon.e: From Middle Dutch bône, from Old Dutch *bōna, from Proto-Germanic *baunō.

examples: Zij heeft een zoon gebaard.

baren.e: From Middle Dutch baren. The a in the stem and the weak inflection are unetymological, but their origin is unknown. The form developed from and displaced the older strong beren (still found in modern ontberen), from Old Dutch beran, from Proto-Germanic *beraną. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European...

bots.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

Bosnia and Herzegovina (a country on the Balkan Peninsula in Southeast Europe)

barman.e: From bar + man, a partial calque of English barman.

1900, Frederik van Eeden, Van de koele meren des Doods, W. Versluys (publ., 1st ed.), page 9.

bekoring.e: From Middle Dutch becoringe. Equivalent to bekoren + -ing.

nakedness, especially as art subject or in porn

bloot.e: From Middle Dutch bloot, from Old Dutch *blōt, from Proto-West Germanic *blaut, from Proto-Germanic *blautaz. Cognate with German bloß, Old English blēat, Old Norse blautr.

inflection of best, the superlative degree of goed:

De aardbeving veroorzaakte een grote breuk in de aardkorst.

breuk.e: From Middle Dutch broke, brueke, from Old Dutch *bruki, from Proto-West Germanic *bruki, from Proto-Germanic *brukiz.

bruid.e: From Middle Dutch bruut, from Old Dutch *brūd, from Proto-West Germanic *brūdi, from Proto-Germanic *brūdiz. Compare German Braut, West Frisian breid, English bride, Danish brud.

bahrein.e: Ultimately from Arabic الْبَحْرَيْن (al-baḥrayn, “Bahrain; the two seas”), with omission of the definite article.

biecht.e: From Middle Dutch biechte, from Old Dutch bigihti.

boomslang.e: Compound of boom + slang. Attested from the mid-18th century.

mayor, burgomaster (head of a city or municipality)

one of two species of gull:

burgemeester.e: From Middle Dutch borchmeester, borgermeester. This form represents a compound of burg (“burgh, town”) + meester (“master”), but forms with -r- are influenced by burger (“citizen”). The earliest attestations lack the -r-.

s.def: to threaten (to pose a risk to)
to threaten (to express a threat to)
everyday occupation, daily concern, daily business (that which occupies someone on an everyday basis)

besogne.e: From Middle Dutch besoenge, from Old French besoign.

s.def: Bethlehem (a city in the West Bank, Palestine)
a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

bethlehem.e:
From Latin Bēthlehēmum, Bēthleëm, from Ancient Greek Βηθλεέμ (Bēthleém), from Hebrew בֵּית לֶחֶם (bet léchem, “house of bread”).
The hamlet is first attested as Bethlehem in 1502 and is named after a former monastery, which was named in turn after the city in Palestine.

bevel.e:
From Middle Dutch bevel. By surface analysis, deverbal from bevelen.
Compare German Befehl.

belladonna.e: Borrowed from Italian belladonna (bella donna, literally “beautiful lady”), altered by folk etymology from Medieval Latin blādōna (“nightshade”), from Gaulish *blātōnā, blātunā, from Proto-Celtic *blātus (“flower”), from Proto-Indo-European *bʰléh₃tus, from *bʰleh₃- (“blossom, flower”).

blok.e: From Middle Dutch blok, from Old Dutch *blok, from Proto-Germanic *blukką.

s.def: to mix in a blender, to blend

blenden.e: From Middle Dutch blenden, from Old Dutch *blenden, from Proto-Germanic *blandijaną.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Bergeijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

bucht.e: First attested as Bucht in 1936. Derived from a dialectal variant of bocht, used here in the sense "fenced-in property, enclosed parcel". Compare De Bocht and Buchten.

s.def: a tax that was presented to lower-level governments as a petition for a lump sum; raising the tax was left to the lower-level governments

bede.e: From Middle Dutch bede, from Old Dutch beda. See the verb bidden.

s.def: a village in Land van Cuijk, North Brabant, Netherlands
to ensure the sustainability of a particular policy or procedure

borgen.e: From Middle Dutch borgen. Equivalent to borg + -en.

s.def: a male given name, equivalent to English Bert

examples: Voor dit gerecht wordt de bloemkool geroerbakt met kruiden.

s.def: large breast, melon, bazonga (often modified with adjectives or phrases indicating a large size)
examples: Ik heb hele grote bloemkolen.

bloemkool.e: From bloem + kool, calque of Italian cavolfiore or calque of Middle French chou-fleur, which also comes from Italian cavolfiore.

bloesem.e: From Middle Dutch bloesem, bloeseme, (also bloesene), from Old Dutch *bluostmo, from Proto-Germanic *blōstmô. Compare Old English blostma ( > English blossom), Middle Low German blossem, blosem (“blossom”), Middle High German bluost (“blossom”). More at blossom.

s.def: someone skilled in finding people; skilled or persistent tracker or stalker

bloedhond.e: From bloed (“blood”) + hond (“dog, hound”). The use for a scenthound and the figurative senses (16th c.) are native; compare German Bluthund, Middle Low German blôthunt. The use for the specific breed (late 19th c.) after English bloodhound.

bloemlezing.e: From bloem (“flower”) + lezing (“reading”), a calque of Latin florilegium, itself a calque of Ancient Greek ἀνθολογία (anthología), from ἄνθος (ánthos, “flower”) + -λογία (-logía, “reading”).

beton.e: Borrowed from French béton (“concrete”), from Latin bitūmen (“asphalt”), see there for more.

basalt.e: Borrowed from French basalte, from Middle French basalte, from Late Latin basaltes, a misspelling of Latin basanites, from Ancient Greek βασανίτης (basanítēs), from βάσανος (básanos, “touchstone”), from Egyptian bḫn (“a hard stone”).

s.def: to pole (push a craft forward with a pole)
to land on a tree (of a pheasant)

bron.e:
From earlier born, from Middle Dutch borne, Old Dutch brunno, from Proto-West Germanic *brunnō, from Proto-Germanic *brunnô.
The inherited word underwent metathesis in Middle Dutch, but this was undone through influence from German Brunnen.

buks.e: Borrowed from German Büchse. Doublet of box, bus (“container”), and pyxis.

s.def: a beech, tree of the genus Fagus

examples: Hij gaf de deur een harde beuk met zijn schouder.

beuk.e: From eastern Middle Dutch boke, boeke, from Old Dutch *buoka, from Proto-West Germanic *bōkijā. Western varieties of Dutch, including generally Standard Dutch, do not have umlaut of long vowels. The eastern variety with umlaut was probably adopted to avoid homonymy with boek.

bidder.e: From Middle Dutch biddere. Equivalent to bidden + -er.

s.def: Breda (a city and municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands)

breda.e: From Middle Dutch brêdâ; first attested as brida in 1152. Etymology uncertain. Possibly from breed (“broad”) and aa (“river, watercourse”), but the stress placement is unexpected (compare Gouda).

brein.e:
From Middle Dutch brein, next to the more common bragen, bregen, from Old Dutch *bragan, *bregin, from Proto-West Germanic *bragn, from Proto-Germanic *bragną.
Cognate with West Frisian brein, Low German Bregen, Brägen, English brain.

beloven.e: From Middle Dutch beloven. Equivalent to be- + loven.

bolder.e: From French poltre, from Medieval Latin pulliter, from Latin pullus. Influenced by the Dutch word bol (“sphere, globe”).

bajes.e: Borrowed from Yiddish בית (bajes, “house”), from Hebrew בית (báyit, “house”).

bajen.e: Variant of baden with d-weakening; compare rooie, rode (and, with hypercorrection, kade, kaai).

s.def: to be annoyed, frustrated or fed up
examples: Niemand vond het erg dat de kelner de verkeerde aanspreektitel gebruikte, maar hij baalt wel van de vergissing.
Dat de brug nu al weken kapot is, is balen.

s.def: to be bored; to be mildly annoyed through having nothing to do
examples: Hij baalde thuis dus hij ging buiten de stad om te gaan zwemmen.

balen.e: From baal (“bale”) + -en or rather balen (“bales”), used in ironic expressions with tabak (“tobacco; something excellent”) to express frustration or annoyance; compare tabak hebben van. The reanalysis as a verb probably developed from phrases like het is balen (“it is frustrating”).

examples: Het mocht niet baten.

baten.e: From Middle Dutch baten, from Old Dutch *baton, from Proto-West Germanic *batēn, from Proto-Germanic *batāną, from the same root as beter.

s.def: a raised platform, often with a flight of steps, that lead into a building
an intermediate platform between flights of stairs, which normally involves a change in direction; a landing
a balcony (open or roofed), porch or lean-to
examples:
1960, Peter Koelewijn, "Kom van dat dak af", Peter en zijn Rockets, Kom van dat dak af.
Jan Janssen z'n vrouw was een koordanseres, / maar bij gebrek aan een touw klom ze op het bordes.

s.def: to knock the seeds out of a flax plant

boten.e: From Middle Dutch boten, from Old Dutch *bōtan, from Proto-West Germanic *bautan, from Proto-Germanic *bautaną (“to strike”).

s.def: turn (a chance to use (something) shared in sequence with others)
examples: Wie is er aan de beurt?
Het is uw beurt.
Hij bracht zijn brommer in voor een beurt.
Hij of zij heeft een goeie beurt nodig.
Hij of zij heeft lang geen beurt gehad.

beurt.e: From Middle Dutch boerte. Equivalent to beuren + -te.

examples: Heb jij een briefje van vijftig bij je?

s.def: to shop, to do the shopping (at a grocery store or supermarket, not for luxury items)

examples: Ik denk, dus ik besta.
Aardolie is een fossiele brandstof bestaande uit ketens van koolwaterstoffen.

bestaan.e: From Middle Dutch bestaen. Equivalent to be- + staan.

examples: Hé buschauffeur, / de hele bus moet plassen.

bezant.e: From Middle Dutch besant, from Old French bezant, from Latin byzantius.

s.def: load, a person's relevant (especially hindering) background

bagage.e: Borrowed from Middle French bagage. First attested in the 16th century.

examples: En het bedrijf bood me mijn huidige baan in Nederland aan.

s.def: act, major unit in a theatre piece

bedrijf.e: From Middle Dutch bedrijf. Equivalent to a deverbal from bedrijven. Compare German Betrieb.

examples: Ik neem biefstuk met patat, dat is ook echt Vlaams.
Bak de biefstuk in de pan vlug bruin. Gebruik een vork om de biefstuk in de pan te leggen.

biefstuk.e: From earlier beefsteak, from English beefsteak, with the second part of the compound changed by analogy (through folk etymology) with names of other pieces of meat in Dutch ending in -stuk.

betel.e: Borrowed from Portuguese bétele \ bétel, from Malayalam വെറ്റില (veṟṟila).

examples: Oorspronkelijk was het Atomium bedoeld om slechts gedurende de Expo overeind te blijven, maar het bleek dermate populair dat het nooit is afgebroken.

blijken.e: From Middle Dutch bliken, from Old Dutch *blīcan, from Proto-West Germanic *blīkan, from Proto-Germanic *blīkaną (“to shine, gleam”), *blīkijaną.

bijwoord.e: From Middle Dutch biwoort. Equivalent to bij + woord.

examples: Besteed je veel geld aan kleren?

besteden.e: From Middle Dutch besteden, bestaden. Equivalent to be- + steden.

bericht.e: From Middle Dutch berecht. Equivalent to a verbal neutrum from berichten.

bijbel.e: From Middle Dutch bibele, from Latin Biblia, from Ancient Greek βιβλία (biblía, “books”), plural of βιβλίον (biblíon, “book”).

balling.e: From Middle Dutch ballinc, assimilated from banlinc, which consists of ban + -linc. Doublet of banneling.

s.def: brigadier, corporal, or a colonel in charge of a brigade
Byzantium (an ancient Greek city situated on the Bosporus in modern Turkey, renamed Constantinople in 330 C.E.; modern Istanbul)

byzantium.e: Borrowed from Latin Bȳzantium, from Ancient Greek Βῡζᾰ́ντῐον (Būzắntĭon).

examples: Dat is bepaald door de wet.

s.def: to determine, to decide, to choose
to limit oneself, to restrain oneself, to contain oneself
examples: In de toekomst zal ik proberen mezelf tot slechts twee per dag te bepalen.

bepalen.e: From Middle Dutch bepalen (“to put poles on/around”). Equivalent to paal + be- -en.

s.def: to decide, to make a decision
examples: Ik heb besloten om te stoppen met roken

besluiten.e: From Middle Dutch besluten, from Old Dutch *bislūtan. Equivalent to be- + sluiten.

examples: Twaalfjarig Bestand

s.def: capable of withstanding, capable to withstand
examples: Dit huis is bestand tegen stormen.

bestand.e: From Middle Dutch bestant. Equivalent to be- + stand, compare German Bestand.

examples: Hij kan nu niet praten. Hij is bezig.
Snel! De film is al bezig!

bezig.e: From Middle Dutch bēsich, from Old Dutch *bisig, from Proto-West Germanic *bisīg.

s.def: a type of coat or jacket
a buss, a type of fishing vessel

buis.e: From Middle Dutch buse, of uncertain origin, plausibly representing an umlauted byform *beusju of Proto-West Germanic *beusu (“rush, bentgrass”) which gives Dutch bies. For the "television" sense, compare English tube.

s.def: to think of, to make up
to reconsider, to change one's mind

bedenken.e: From Middle Dutch bedenken, from Old Dutch *bithenkan, *bithenkon, from Proto-West Germanic *biþankijan, from Proto-Germanic *biþankijaną. Equivalent to be- + denken.

bevrijden.e: From earlier bevrijen, from Middle Dutch bevriën, a prefixed form of vriën (“to free”). Can be interpreted synchronically as be- + vrijen or vrij + be- -en. The -d- is a hypercorrection, probably influenced by the past tense bevrijd and/or compensating for a mistakenly supposed case of d-weakenin...

examples: Ik heb moeite met het invullen van mijn belastingformulier.
Je moet je belastingformulier voor april indienen.
Deze brug kan een belasting van maximaal 10 ton aan.
De mentale belasting van het werk kan soms overweldigend zijn.
Veel mensen klagen over hoge belastingen in hun land.
De overheid heeft nieuwe belastingen ingevoerd op luxegoederen.
De generator kan een belasting van 500 kilowatt aan.
Bij overmatige belasting kan het circuit uitvallen.

belasting.e: From Middle Dutch belastinge (“burden, accusation”). Equivalent to belasten (“to load, burden”) + -ing.

s.def: to charge (e.g. taxes), to tax or levy
to charge, to order, to invest

belasten.e: From Middle Dutch belasten. Equivalent to last + be- -en.

baco.e: A compound of the initial syllables of bacardi cola, from Bacardi (a brand of rum - although any brand of rum may be used in a baco) and cola. Such clipped compounds are common in informal usage; compare for example vrijmibo, esma, gito.

examples: Ik heb geen zwembroek bij me. Bovendien is het water veel te koud.
Agenten snapten de kersverse automobilist en pakten zijn rijbewijs af. Bovendien kreeg de snelheidsduivel een flinke boete.

bezoeken.e: From Middle Dutch besoeken, from Old Dutch bisuoken. Equivalent to be- + zoeken.

examples: De postbode zal het pakketje morgen bezorgen bij uw huis.
Het restaurant kan een heerlijke maaltijd bezorgen voor uw feest.

s.def: to provide for, to care for

bezorgen.e: From Middle Dutch besorgen, from Old Dutch *bisorgon, from Proto-West Germanic *bisorgōn. Equivalent to be- + zorgen.

s.def: to do, to perform, to carry out

bedrijven.e: From Middle Dutch bedriven. Equivalent to be- + drijven.

s.def: to arrive at, to reach, to get to

bereiken.e: From Middle Dutch bereiken. Equivalent to be- + reiken.

s.def: to intend, to have an aim
to mean, to intend to communicate

examples: In Nederland heb je op het moment geen echte boulevardbladen zoals in Duitsland of Engeland, maar dat kan natuurlijk veranderen.

s.def: all foreign countries, collectively; anything abroad
examples: Zij studeert in het buitenland.

s.def: everything within the boundaries of a nation (as opposed to buitenland)
examples: de binnenlandpagina in de krant: the national news section in the newspaper
Deze artiest heeft succes in binnen- en buitenland.
Ze is momenteel in het binnenland.

binnenland.e: From binnen + land. Perhaps backformed from binnenlands.

s.def: bishop (official in Orthodox, Catholic and some Protestant churches overseeing a diocese)

bisschop.e:
From Middle Dutch bischop, from Old Dutch biskop, from Latin episcopus, from Ancient Greek ἐπίσκοπος (epískopos). The use of the term for mulled wine probably derived from English or German.
The chess sense is likely a semantic loan from English bishop.

s.def: Berlin (the capital and largest city of Germany)
Berlin (a state of Germany, containing the capital city)
a settlement and former plantation in Para, Suriname

berlijn.e: Perhaps borrowed from German Berlin. Insofar as the name has undergone diphthongisation it must be of some age; hence possibly inherited from Middle Dutch [Term?], from Middle Low German [Term?]. The city was of relevance since circa 1250.

s.def: a budget, projected income and spending of a public or private organisation in a given period
a budget, the funds (or other means) reserved for a project

begroting.e: From Middle Dutch begrotinge. Equivalent to begroten + -ing.

s.def: to name, to call by someone's or something's name

benoemen.e: From Middle Dutch benoemen. Equivalent to be- + noemen.

examples: De schijnbare beweging van de zon langs de hemel is een gevolg van de draaiing van de Aarde.
De dokter heeft gezegd dat ik te weinig beweging krijg.

s.def: movement, group, organisation (people with a common ideology or goal)
examples: De beweging voor homo-rechten is gaandeweg steeds invloedrijker geworden.
Het is de taak van de regering om beleid te maken.
Het beleid van ons hotel ten aanzien van honden staat in deze folder.
Het schilderen van een plafond moet je met beleid doen.

beleid.e: From Middle Dutch beleit. Equivalent to a deverbal from beleiden.

bereidheid.e: From Middle Dutch bereitheit. Equivalent to bereid + -heid.

betekenen.e: From Middle Dutch betekenen, from Old Dutch *bitēknon, from Proto-West Germanic *bitaiknijan. Equivalent to be- + tekenen. Cognates include German bezeichnen and English betoken.

s.def: briefing (short and concise summary of a situation)

bliksem.e: From Middle Dutch blexeme, from Old Dutch blikisni (“lightning bolts”), ultimately from a Proto-Germanic derivation of Proto-Indo-European *bʰleyǵ-.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Haarlemmermeer, North Holland, Netherlands

bornholm.e: Borrowed from Danish Bornholm. Named after a former farmhouse which was named in turn after the Danish island.

boodschap.e: From Middle Dutch bodescap, from Old Dutch *bodaskap, from Proto-West Germanic *bodaskapi. Equivalent to bode + -schap.

s.def: to limit, to curtail, to restrict, to constrain
examples: De vrijheid van meningsuiting wordt niet alleen door de wet beperkt, maar ook door normen van moraal en beschaving.
Voor de verwoestijning van het schiereiland was dit het kerngebied van verschillende oude beschavingen.

s.def: civilization (society, as opposed to wild nature)
examples: De schipbreukelingen wisten de beschaving te bereiken.
Een recht om te beledigen bestaat wel, onderstreepte de koningin.

beledigen.e: From leed + be- -igen, a calque of German beleidigen. Unrelated to ledig.

beschimpen.e: From Middle Dutch beschimpen. Equivalent to be- + schimpen.

s.def: to have mastery over, to know (how to)
examples: Beheers je al wat Nederlands?

beheersen.e: From be- + heersen. Compare German beherrschen.

examples: Ik heb de neiging om alles te bewaren. Ik kan echt niets weggooien. Daarom staan er zo veel spullen in de garage.

bewaren.e: From Middle Dutch bewaren, from Old Dutch biwaron, from Proto-West Germanic *biwarōn, equivalent to be- + waren. Cognate with English beware.

s.def: to shut out, to lock out
examples: Ik heb per ongeluk mijn sleutels binnen gelaten en mezelf buitengesloten.
Ze voelde zich buitengesloten omdat haar vrienden haar niet uitnodigden voor het feest.

brandweer.e: From brand (“fire”) + weer (“defence”), calque of German Feuerwehr.

s.def: a bier, a stretcher, a litter; a device used to carry someone or something, especially wounded or dead people
a bed on which a dead person is displayed before he is buried
examples: Wanneer ik stierf en zij die mij beminden / Rondom mijn baar staan en de een d’andre vraagt:

s.def: a bar, an ingot (of gold or another metal)

examples: Ulisses zworf weleer op wilde woeste baren,/ Minerves wreeden wrok en wrange wraek ten doel,
Ik heb geen baar geld bij me.

1930 August 3, Si Omong, "Baren en... baren.", Algemeen Handelsblad, ochtendblad, page 12.
Een leergierige baar wil gedurende het eerste etmaal van zijn verblijf op Java alles zien, alles weten, alles proeven.

Bovendien werden de baren daardoor in korten tijd scheeps- en »marine«-wijs gemaakt, leerden de taal en de gebruiken van hun nieuwe wereld en praatten in weinig tijds mee als de besten over »snerfnimf« en »galjoenkapitein«, over »pluimgraaf« en »waschteef« zowel als over »Droge«, »Puist« en »Poen«, over »Clovis« en »Bakkertje« en over de »fielten« en »bokken« hunner dagelijksche omgeving.

baar.e: From Middle Dutch bâre, from Old Dutch bier, from Proto-West Germanic *bērō, from Proto-Germanic *bērō, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰer- (“to carry, bear”). Cognate with Saterland Frisian Beere (“stretcher, bier”), English bier, German Bahre (“bier, stretcher”).

s.def: to make an offer, to bid

bieden.e: From Middle Dutch bieden, from Old Dutch biedan, from Proto-West Germanic *beudan (“to offer”), from Proto-Germanic *beudaną (“to offer”), from Proto-Indo-European *bʰewdʰ- (“be awake, aware”).

bloeddruk.e: Compound of bloed (“blood”) + druk (“pressure”). Compare German Blutdruck, Swedish blodtryck, Danish blodtryk.

examples: het algemeen belang

belang.e: From Middle Dutch belanc, from belangen. Compare English belong.

examples: Hij is een bekend acteur in Nederland.
De Eiffeltoren is het bekendste monument van Parijs.
Haar gezicht was me bekend, maar ik kon haar naam niet herinneren.
Dit merk is bekend en vertrouwd door velen.

bekend.e: From Middle Dutch bekent, past participle of bekennen (“to know”).

s.def: an acquaintance, someone known/trusted to the person of reference, yet often less than a friend
The known, what one is familiar with
examples: Het bekende lijkt meestal veiliger maar saaier.

bevelhebber.e: From bevel (“command”) + hebben (“to have”) + -er. First known attestations from ca. 1535 and thus perhaps after German Befehl(s)haber (1490), probably already Middle High German bevelhaber (1335, copy 16th c.).

s.def: to manipulate, to try change opinion

bewerken.e: From Middle Dutch bewerken, from Old Dutch bewirken. Equivalent to be- + werken.

blinken.e: From Middle Dutch blinken, from Old Dutch *blinkan, from Proto-Germanic *blinkaną.

barmhartig.e: From Middle Dutch barmhertich, from Middle High German barmherzec, Old High German barmherzi, calque from Latin misericors. Equivalent to barmen (see erbarmen) + hart + -ig.

bazen.e: From Middle Dutch basen, from Old Dutch *bason, from Proto-West Germanic *basōn.

examples: een gezellige bedoeling
Hij is beroepsmatig fotograaf.
Zij doet beroepsmatig onderzoek naar klimaatverandering.

bijknippen.e: From bij (indicates repetition of an action) + knippen (“to cut”).

betasten.e: From Middle Dutch betasten. Equivalent to be- + tasten.

s.def: a complete mess (mess as if resulting from animals stampeding)

bekendmaken.e: From bekend + maken, cf. German bekanntmachen.

examples: Het bestuur van het bedrijf besloot om nieuwe strategieën te implementeren.
De leden van het bestuur zijn verantwoordelijk voor de besluitvorming binnen de organisatie.
Het bestuur van de koning was lang en welvarend.
Hij bestuurt het schip met precisie door de smalle grachten.
De monteur bestuurt de machines in de fabriek.

s.def: to drive (e.g. a car or vehicle)
examples: Ze bestuurt al jarenlang vrachtwagens over lange afstanden.
De president bestuurt het land met een sterke leiderschapsstijl.

besturen.e: From be- + sturen. Arose as a Northern Dutch variant of bestieren.

betonen.e: From Middle Dutch betonen. Equivalent to be- + tonen.

bleek.e: From Middle Dutch blêec, from Old Dutch *blēk, from Proto-West Germanic *blaik, from Proto-Germanic *blaikaz.

examples: Hij heeft een grote buik.

s.def: paunch (referring euphemistically to a protrusive belly)
the lowest inner part of a ship's hull, where water accumulates
examples: De zeilboot heeft water in de buik.

buik.e: From Middle Dutch buuc, from Old Dutch būc, from Proto-West Germanic *būk, from Proto-Germanic *būkaz.

beveiligen.e: From veilig + be- -en, replacing older beveilen.

s.def: ally (member of the same alliance; one who offers support when needed)
examples: Nederland en België waren bondgenoten tijdens de oorlog.
De twee landen hebben een langdurige geschiedenis als bondgenoten in tijden van nood.

s.def: fellow member of a federation, confederacy, association, etc.

bondgenoot.e: From bond + genoot, calque of German Bundesgenosse.

s.def: birch, tree of the genus Betula

berk.e: From Middle Dutch berke, from Old Dutch *berca, from Proto-West Germanic *berku, from Proto-Germanic *berkō, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰerHǵós.

bewoner.e: From Middle Dutch bewōonre. Equivalent to bewonen + -er.

betrekken.e: From Middle Dutch betrecken. Equivalent to be- + trekken.

s.def: to attend, to be present at

examples: Er is daarnaast de mogelijkheid om het apparaat aan de muur te bevestigen.
Ik kan dat gerucht niet bevestigen.

bevestigen.e: From Middle Dutch bevestigen, replacing older bevesten. By surface analysis, be- + vestigen.

examples: De buitenkant van het gebouw is blauw geschilderd.
aan de buitenkant

s.def: rescue, salvage (act of recovering or bringing to safety)
nautical salvage business, rescue company, recovery company (for ships)

bestrijden.e: From Middle Dutch bestriden. Equivalent to be- + strijden.

blussen.e: From Middle Dutch blusschen, from earlier bleschen. Equivalent to be- + lessen, but with loss of the unstressed vowel of the unstressed syllable.

s.def: a buffalo, one of some mammals of the subfamily Bovinae, especially of the genus Bubalus or species Syncerus caffer

buffel.e: From Middle Dutch buffel, from Old French buffle, from Italian bufalo (archaic buffalo), from Latin bubalus, from Ancient Greek βούβαλος (boúbalos).

examples: Die boot stinkt als een bunzing.

bunzing.e: From Middle Dutch bunsinc, bonsinc, also boesinc, businc. Further etymology unknown, not found outside the Ingvaeonic language area. Probably a derivative of the dialectal term boes (“stable”) with the now-unproductive suffix -ing (“originating from, inhabitant of”).

s.def: a bilberry; a plant of the genus Vaccinium
a bilberry, fruit of a plant in this genus, particularly that of Vaccinium myrtillus

bezigheid.e: From Middle Dutch bēsicheit. Equivalent to bezig + -heid.

bijouterie.e: Borrowed from French bijouterie. Equivalent to bijou + -erie.

examples: Een Beloega/ van vier perſoonen op een burrie gedragen.

beloega.e: Borrowed from Russian белу́га (belúga) (in turn бе́лый (bélyj, “white”)). In modern standard Russian белу́га (belúga) means the fish only (the "European sturgeon") and белу́ха (belúxa) a "white whale".

s.def: bison, bovid of the genus Bison

bizon.e: Borrowed from French bison, from Latin bisōn, from Proto-Germanic *wisundaz.

s.def: coypu, member of the family Myocastoridae

bultrug.e: Either from bult (“hump”) + rug (“back”) or a shortening of bultrugwalvis.

boog.e: From Middle Dutch bōge, from Old Dutch bogo, from Proto-West Germanic *bogō, from Proto-Germanic *bugô.

beschouwen.e: From Middle Dutch beschouwen. Equivalent to be- + schouwen.

s.def: to deal with, address or discuss as a subject

bepaling.e: From Middle Dutch bepalinge. Equivalent to bepalen + -ing.

examples: Ik heb dat besluit niet overhaast genomen.

s.def: determination (to do or follow through with something)
a hamlet in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

basse.e: First attested as bassinghe around 1425. Etymology unknown. The oldest attestation of the toponym is a derivation from Old Dutch Basse (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-, but the loss of the collectivising suffix in later attestations is strange.

examples: Hij boog zich weer naar voren.

buigen.e: From Middle Dutch bugen, from Old Dutch *būgan, from Proto-West Germanic *beugan, from Proto-Germanic *būganą, a variant form of *beuganą.

s.def: an executioner, torturer, one who carries out executions and other judicial corporal punishments
a cruel person, in act or sadistic streak

beul.e: From Middle Dutch buel, earlier buedel, from Old Dutch *budil, from Proto-West Germanic *budil, from Proto-Germanic *budilaz. Doublet of pedel.

s.def: cover for moving joints, as in articulated buses

balg.e: From Middle Dutch balch, from Old Dutch balg, from Proto-West Germanic *balgi, from Proto-Germanic *balgiz.

bezit.e: From Middle Dutch besit. Equivalent to a deverbal from bezitten.

brouwerij.e: From brouwen (“to brew”) + -erij (“-ery”) or alternatively brouwer (“brewer”) + -ij (“-y”). See also the usage notes at -erij.

s.def: a surname originating as an occupation
examples: Intuitionism is a creation of the Dutch mathematician, philosopher, poet and man of wisdom L.E.J. Brouwer (1881-1966).

bolero.e: Borrowed from Spanish bolero. The clothing senses may have been borrowed via French boléro.

bami.e: Borrowed from Hokkien 肉麵 /肉面 (bah-mī) or indirectly via Indonesian bakmi.

s.def: a charm (small trinket or pendant on a bracelet or necklace)
obsolete form of pedel (“beagle, academic usher”)
bacterium, any member of the domain Bacteria

bacterie.e: Dutchification of older bacterium, from New Latin bactērium, from Ancient Greek βακτήριον (baktḗrion, “small staff”), from βακτηρία (baktēría).

baken.e: From Middle Dutch baken, from Old Frisian bāken. Displaced Middle Dutch boken, from Old Dutch *bōkan. Both forms originate from Proto-Germanic *baukną.

s.def: wine (in recent texts only used of red wine, and in earlier texts also of wines from the German Rhineland)
examples:
1870, P. J. van der Noordaa, "Levenslust", in D. F. Tersteeg, Nederland, vol. 1, J. C. Loman (publ.), page 348.
»Ja, een half fleschjen rooie baai van een krachtig merk.”

s.def: a neighbourhood of Etten-Leur, North Brabant, Netherlands

baai.e: Directly or ultimately from Middle French baie.

s.def: diminutive of beer (all senses and etymologies)
a male guinea pig, Cavia porcellus
a pile of flat brick layers

beschermer.e: From Middle Dutch beschermere. Equivalent to beschermen + -er.

s.def: to tar, to cover with tar

beteren.e: From Middle Dutch bēteren, from Old Dutch *beteron, from Proto-Germanic *batizōną. Equivalent to beter + -en.

s.def: extraordinarily, extremely, very (used as an intensifier)

examples: Het ijs begint hier en daar te barsten.

s.def: to suffer and/or break under emotional pressure
to urge, be near emotional eruption
examples: Hij staat op barsten.

barsten.e: From Middle Dutch bersten, barsten, from metathesis of Old Dutch brestan, from Proto-West Germanic *brestan, from Proto-Germanic *brestaną, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰres- (“to burst, break, crack, split, separate”).

biologie.e: Borrowed from German Biologie, from Ancient Greek βιολογία (biología). This is a compound of βίος (bíos, “life”) and λόγος (lógos, “reason”) with the suffix -ία (-ía) (forms abstracta), equivalent to bio- + -logie.

s.def: brook, creek (running waterway smaller than a river)
a village and municipality of Limburg, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Bree, Belgium
a village and former municipality of Berg en Dal, Gelderland, Netherlands
a village in Montferland, Gelderland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Venray, Limburg, Netherlands

beek.e: From Middle Dutch bēke, from Old Dutch beki, from Proto-Germanic *bakiz.

bijl.e: From Middle Dutch bile, from Old Dutch and Frankish *billi, *bīal, *bīala, from Proto-Germanic *bilją (“axe”). Compare German Beil.

s.def: a receptacle in which a fuel or candle is placed to be burnt

examples:
P.C. Hooft, Nederlandsche Historien, Book XXII, in W. Hellinga en P. Tuynman (eds.), Pieter Corneliszoon Hooft, Alle de gedrukte werken, 1611-1738, vol. 5, page 968.
Treslong, hebbende, korts te voore, met den Heere van Warmondt, Ammiraal van Hollandt, en de Scheepshopluiden der vloote by den Doel, zeekeren aanslagh, tot bevryding der Schelde, beraamt, verzocht drie oft vier lichte scheepen van oorlogh, om te dienen tot geleiders aan die van Antwerpen; twintigh schouwen elk met acht roeyers, om volk te landen; drie galeyen, elke van vyftigh voeten kiels, om op de stroom en elders gebruikt te worden; eenighe branders, om 't maaken van 's vyands brug te verhinderen, vier boots die tot Vlissinge laaghen; voorts etlyke bylen, voorhaamers, spaaden, en andre behoeften.

Gedurende een hevigen storm bij het eiland Porto Santo, die o. a. een ten anker liggend schip van zijn ankers had geslagen en in de hevige branders te gronde had doen gaan, kwam eene boot in het midden der baar aanzeilen, koersende naar den wal.

s.def: A buzzard, any bird of prey of the genus Buteo.

buizerd.e: From earlier buysard, buzart, from Middle French busard, from Old French buison, busart, from Latin būteō.

boei.e: From Middle Dutch boeye, from Old French boie (“fetter, chain, clog”), from Latin boia (“(leather) collar, band, fetter”).

examples: Vroeger werden allerlei soort groenten opgediend, daarbij wist men nog: het is van mijn eigen grondje. Tegenwoordig alleen boulanger en kouseband en daar zorgt de leverancier in de stad voor.

boulanger.e: Pseudo-Gallicism, from French beringène, from Spanish berenjena, from Arabic بَاذِنْجَان (bāḏinjān), from Persian بادنجان (bâdenjân). Compare Antilles French bélangère (“aubergine”) and Antillean Creole bélanjè (“aubergine”). Doublet of aubergine.

bodemloos.e: From Middle Dutch bodemloos. Equivalent to bodem (“bottom”) + -loos (“-less”).

s.def: a bird in the hand is worth two in the bush

beter een vogel in de hand dan tien in d.i: /ˌbeː.tər ˈeːn ˈvoː.ɣəl ɪn də ˈɦɑnt ˌdɑn ˈtin ɪn də ˈlʏxt/
beter een vogel in de hand dan tien in d.e: Literally, “better one bird in the hand than ten in the sky”.
f: beter één vogel in de hand (canonical)

s.def: biomass (vegetation or other organic matter used as fuel)

examples: de bewuste dag – the very day, the day in question
Dit streven naar bepaalde effecten gebeurt bewust.

s.def: a beret, type of cap, also used as part some military uniforms

baret.e: From Middle French barrete, from dialectal Italian baretta.

bevelen.e: From Middle Dutch bevēlen, from Old Dutch bifelan, from Proto-West Germanic *bifelhan.

examples: De varkens wroeten in de bagger.

s.def: filth, muck, any mucky or dirty substance (such as dredge)

examples: Wat voor bagger heb je nou gekocht?
Het weer is bagger vandaag.

bagger.e: From Middle Dutch baggaerds, of uncertain origin, but possibly a late Indo-European substrate borrowing shared with Proto-Slavic *bagno (“silt, peat, mud”).

s.def: the spoil, booty taken by violence, as in war
the gains, as in a game of chance

buit.e: 16th century, borrowed from Middle Low German büte, whence also German Beute and eventually all other related forms. Of uncertain ultimate origin; possibly a Celtic borrowing, from Proto-Celtic *boudi (“victory, booty, spoils”). If so, related to the name of Boudica, a British Celtic queen.

s.def: service industry worker (as opposed to manual labourers)

bediende.e: Inflected form of bediend, calque of German Bedienter.

s.def: order (request for some product or service; a commission to purchase, sell, or supply goods)
examples: een bestelling plaatsen

bamboe.e:
Etymology tree
Proto-Dravidian *wē
Proto-Dravidian *mpu
Proto-Dravidian *wēmpu
Tamil வேம்பு (vēmpu)bor.
▲
Proto-Dravidian *wēmpu
Kannada ಬಂಬು (bambu)bor.
Malay bambubor.
Portuguese bambubor.
Dutch bamboe
Borrowed from Portuguese bambu, from Malay bambu, from Kannada ಬಂಬು (bambu).

s.def: to produce a swelling or secretion
to blush, to redden in the face
to flourish, to be in one's period of maximum activity or influence
examples: Hij bloeide in de laatste twee decennia van de veertiende eeuw.
Hij bloeit als 'n rund!
M'n knie bloeit.

bloeien.e: From Middle Dutch bloeyen, from Old Dutch bluoien, from Proto-West Germanic *blōan, from Proto-Germanic *blōaną.

s.def: to be of assistance to, to serve
to administer the last sacraments to

bedienen.e: From Middle Dutch bedienen. Equivalent to be- + dienen.

s.def: to shake, to shiver, to tremble

beven.e: From Middle Dutch bēven, from Old Dutch *bivon, from Proto-West Germanic *bibēn, from Proto-Germanic *bibāną.

s.def: to evaluate, to assess, to judge
examples: De schaal van Torino is een manier om het inslagrisico van planetoïden en kometen te beoordelen.

s.def: to spoil, to go off, to go bad

bederven.e: From Middle Dutch bederven; see there for further etymology. Unrelated to derven, but related to verderven.

breien.e: From Middle Dutch breiden, from Old Dutch *bregdan, from Proto-Germanic *bregdaną.

s.def: Bonaire (an island, dependent territory, and special municipality of the Netherlands in the Caribbean, off the west coast of Venezuela)

bonaire.e: Uncertain. According to one hypothesis, the toponym derives from the Caquetio word bonay ("low country"). A derivation from Spanish buen (“good”) and aire (“air”) is less likely.

s.def: the upper part, or superstructure, of a building
the upper-level classes of a school, e.g. the 6th, 7th, and 8th grades of grammar school, or the last two school years of high school, in the Netherlands

examples: De betekenis van dit woord is "betekenis" in het Engels.
De betekenis van dit symbool is onbekend.
De betekenis van deze traditie gaat terug tot eeuwen geleden.
De betekenis van deze ontdekking kan de loop van de geschiedenis veranderen.

betekenis.e: From Middle Dutch betêkenesse. Equivalent to betekenen (“to mean”) + -nis.

beheren.e: From Middle Dutch beheren. Equivalent to heer + be- -en.

examples: Die onzinnige bewerking heb ik ongedaan gemaakt.
Delen is de moeilijkste van de vier elementaire bewerkingen.

bezorging.e: From Middle Dutch besorginge. Equivalent to bezorgen + -ing.

beschrijving.e: From Middle Dutch bescrivinge. Equivalent to beschrijven + -ing.

berichten.e: From Middle Dutch berichten. Equivalent to be- + richten.

betreurenswaardig.e: From the gerund of betreuren (“to regret”) + -s- + -waardig.

beseffen.e: From Middle Dutch beseffen, from Old Dutch *biseffen, from bi- + *seffen. The latter part is from Proto-Germanic *sabjaną.

s.def: possessor (and possibly, but not necessarily, also the owner)

bezitter.e: From Middle Dutch besittere. Equivalent to bezitten + -er.

bewijzen.e: From Middle Dutch bewisen. Equivalent to be- + wijzen.

s.def: a bronze; a work made of bronze
examples: De archeologen hebben meerdere Romeinse bronzen opgegraven.

brons.e: Borrowed from Middle French bronze, from Italian bronzo.

examples: Zij zullen ontvangen wat zij ook behoeven.
Je kunt je huis naar behoeven uitbreiden.

behoeven.e: From Middle Dutch behoeven, from Old Dutch *bihuovon, from Proto-Germanic *bihōbōną. Equivalent to be- + hoeven. Cognate with English behove, behoove (“to be necessary for”), obsolete German behufen (cf. Behuf), Danish behøve (“to need”), Norwegian behøve (“to need”), Swedish behöva (“to have use...

examples: Ik heb geen bad thuis, maar gelukkig voorziet het appartement van mijn vriendin in die behoefte.
Gisteravond had ik opeens een ongelofelijke kaasbehoefte, dus heb ik wat fondue gemaakt.
U kunt in het toilet boven uw behoefte doen.

behoefte.e: From Middle Dutch behoefte. Equivalent to behoeven + -te.

s.def: to practice regularly, ongoing: some sport, craft, skill, or profession

bescheiden.e: From Middle Dutch bescheiden. Past participle of bescheiden, equivalent to be- + scheiden (“to separate”).

beschrijven.e: From Middle Dutch beschriven. Equivalent to be- + schrijven.

bezoeker.e: From Middle Dutch besoeker. Equivalent to bezoeken + -er.

s.def: to claim, to assert, to allege

beweren.e:
From a merger of two Middle Dutch verbs:
* Middle Dutch beweren, an eastern variant of bewaren (“to prove”), from waer (“true”) (modern waar). Equivalent to waar + be- -en.
* Middle Dutch beweren, equivalent to be- + weren (“to defend against, to hold back”). Closely related to bewaren (“to uphol...

examples: Deze wet is alleen van toepassing op betaalde diensten.

s.def: a team of young promising players

examples: Mijn vrienden waren bezorgd over mijn gezondheid.
Er was niemand die de vraag van de leraar kon beantwoorden.

s.def: to freeze, to be completely still and immobile

bevriezen.e: From Middle Dutch bevriesen. Equivalent to be- + vriezen.

bij uitstek.e: Calque of French par excellence. Uitstek is a deverbal from uitsteken which is not used in Modern Dutch outside of this expression.

belegeren.e: From leger + be- -en. Related to English beleaguer.

examples: Hij koesterde al lang de begeerte om een eigen bedrijf te starten.
Haar grootste begeerte was om te kunnen reizen en nieuwe culturen te ontdekken.

begeerte.e: From Middle Dutch begeerte, variant of begeerde. Equivalent to begeren + -te.

begeren.e: From Middle Dutch begeren. Equivalent to be- + geren.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Weert, Limburg, Netherlands

examples: Het pasgeboren kalf heeft de biest al op.

Deeze eerste Biest …, als meede de tweede, word altoos aan het jong geworpen kalf te drinken gegeven; dog de derde en vierde, enz. veel door de huislieden gebruikt, om met bijvoeging van meel, enz. pannekoeken en zelfs wafelen van te bakken…

biest.e:
First attested as Biest in 1838-1857. Derived from bies (“rush, Scirpus sp.”) with collectivising suffix -t. The toponym may also refer to the practice of using relatively open locations covered with rushes as meeting places and village squares.
See also Limburgish Beest.

s.def: singular dependent-clause present subjunctive of bijdragen

behoorlijk.e: From Middle Dutch behoorlijc. Equivalent to behoren + -lijk.

s.def: to adjust, to adapt, to amend
examples: Hij moest de hoogte van de stoel bijstellen.
We moeten onze strategie bijstellen op basis van de nieuwe informatie.

behouden.e: From Middle Dutch behouden, from Old Dutch *bihaldan, from Proto-West Germanic *bihaldan (“to keep”). Equivalent to be- + houden.

begrafenis.e:
From begraven (“to bury”) + -nis, with devoicing of the -v-.
In Belgium it's still commonly pronounced as "begravenis".

examples: Is die stoel bezet?

s.def: to occupy (forcefully with military personnel), to conquer

bezetten.e: From Middle Dutch besetten, from Old Dutch *bisetten, from Proto-West Germanic *bisattjan, from Proto-Germanic *bisatjaną. Equivalent to be- + zetten.

bezetting.e: From Middle Dutch besettinge. Equivalent to bezetten + -ing. Compare German Besetzung and Besatzung.

s.def: profession, declaration, expression (act of declaring or expressing)
to express, to profess, to declare

betuigen.e: From Middle Dutch betugen. Equivalent to be- + tuigen.

examples: De steen van Rosette kan worden bezichtigd in het British Museum in Londen, waar hij al sinds 1802 wordt bewaard.

bezichtigen.e: From zicht + be- -igen, replacing earlier bezichten. Compare German besichtigen.

s.def: caning, any beating with a stick
specifically, falaka (oriental beating of the soles of the feet)

bastonnade.e: Borrowed from French bastonnade, from Middle French bastonnade.

s.def: bull (male bovine, or one of certain other male mammals)
great seal affixed to certain documents, notably charters from reigning princes and certain dignitaries

bul.e: From Middle Dutch bulle, from Old Dutch *bullo, from Proto-Germanic *bulô. Cognate with English bull.

bisdom.e: From Middle Dutch bisdom. Equivalent to bis(schop) + -dom.

s.def: biochemistry, the branch of chemistry studying compounds that occur in living organisms and the processes occurring in their metabolism and catabolism

biochemie.e: Equivalent to bio- + chemie (“chemistry”). Probably borrowed.

beschuldigen.e: From Middle Dutch beschuldigen, from Old Dutch *biskuldigon, from Proto-West Germanic *biskuldigōn, equivalent to schuld + be- -igen. Replaced older Middle Dutch beschulden by influence of Middle Dutch schuldich (modern Dutch schuldig). Compare Old English besċyldigian (whence Middle English be-s...

examples: Ze haalde een blikje uit de automaat.
Het enige winkeltje in het dorp verkocht alleen groente in blik.

s.def: sheet metal, tin plate; the metallic material tins are made of, often coated with tin or pewter

blik.e: From Middle Dutch blic, ultimately from the root of blijken (“to appear”).

examples:
2009, De Tandem vzw, Brochure for workshop “Brussen aan het woord”
Beide organisaties realiseren daarom samen deze studiedag, waarin de plaats van brussen in de ondersteuning van gezinnen het centrale thema vormt.

beril.e: From Middle Dutch beril, from Old French beril, from Latin bēryllus, from Ancient Greek βήρυλλος (bḗrullos), from Prakrit, (compare Sanskrit वैडूर्य (vaiḍūrya)), from Dravidian. Doublet of bril.

s.def: Used to indicate that a verb is acting on a direct object (making an intransitive verb into a transitive verb). Always unstressed.
Used to change the direct object of a transitive verb, so that what was previously expressed as an optional prepositional object becomes the direct object and vice versa. Always unstressed.
examples: huizen op een land bouwen → een land met huizen bebouwen

be-.e:
From Middle Dutch be-, bi-, from Old Dutch bi-, be-, from Proto-Germanic *bi-, from Proto-Germanic *bi (“near, by”), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₁epi (“at, near”).
Cognate with Saterland Frisian be- (“be-”), West Frisian be- (“be-”), English be-, German Low German be- (“be-”), German be...

s.def: a birch rod, a rod made from birch twigs
black and blue (so badly bruised that the skin is discolored)
a side effect, a side consequence

examples: Dit bedraagt meer dan twee miljoen euro.
De bouwkosten bedroegen € 125 miljoen.

bedragen.e: From Middle Dutch bedragen. Equivalent to be- + dragen.

s.def: to bark, like a dog (rarely in the sense of shouting)

bassen.e: From Middle Dutch bassen, probably onomatopoeic. Further etymology is unknown, there are no known cognates.

s.def: child, kid, only said of minors, in this sense usually in the plural

blaag.e: Compare western German colloquial or dialectal Blag (“kid”).

s.def: a female given name from Latin, equivalent to English Beatrice
a purse, both as object or capital
a stock exchange or exchange market for goods; also used absolutely for the financial sector
a fair, event or place where specific goods, services or know-how are physically presented and usually traded or bartered, often aimed at collectors; a trade fair
a stipend provided by a foundation or other benefactor for a particular purpose, e.g. a scholarship

beurs.e: From Middle Dutch borse, from Old French borse, from Late Latin bursa, from Ancient Greek βύρσα (búrsa).

s.def: purse-snatcher, cutpurse, pick-pocket, a petty criminal who steals wallets, purses, et cetera
hence metaphorically, somebody charging outrageous prices. "Wat een beurzensnijderij!" (Such price-gouging, such highway robbery.)
cemetery, a ground reserved for graves

begraafplaats.e: Compound of begraven (“to bury”) + plaats (“place, site”).

examples: De hond begroef zijn bot net waar zijn voorganger begraven was.

begraven.e: From Middle Dutch begrāven, from Old Dutch bigravan, from Proto-Germanic *bigrabaną. Equivalent to be- + graven.

s.def: one who barks, usually a canine
to bark, like a canine and certain others species
to grumble, notably said of a hungry stomach
to utter pointless sounds, e.g. protest or argue in vain
a young rascal, a naughty to mischievous kid, usually a tomboy
examples: Rob is een onverbeterlijke bengel.

bengel.e: From Middle Dutch bengel, from Old Dutch *bengil, from Proto-West Germanic *bangil, from Proto-Germanic *bangilaz (“club, stick”). Similar sense development is found in German Bengel (“rascal”). Related to English bang.

bokking.e: From Middle Dutch bocking (itself from bok (“buck”), referencing the foul smell), cognate with Middle High German bockinc.

to fine, penalise financially (an offender or offense)

beboeten.e: From boete + be- -en, or be- + boeten.

blozen.e:
From Middle Dutch blosen, further etymology unclear, but ultimately from Proto-Germanic *blus-, perhaps related to *blasį̄ (“blaze”).
Cognates include Middle Low German blosen, West Frisian bloazje, Old Swedish blusa, and with umlaut also Middle Low German blöschen, Old English blȳsian.

s.def: to man (to supply with staff or crew)
examples: Admiraliteiten waren verantwoordelijk voor het uitrusten en bemannen van schepen.

bloeden.e: From Middle Dutch bloeden, from Old Dutch *bluoden, from Proto-West Germanic *blōdijan, from Proto-Germanic *blōþijaną.

s.def: plan, planning, design (of a journey or a building project)

beeldschoon.e: From beeld (“image, statue”) + schoon (“beautiful”), calque of German bildschön.

s.def: mineral water, such as spa water
a glass or drink of it

examples: Noord-Brabant beslaat een gebied van 4919 vierkante kilometer.
De ruiten besloegen waardoor ik niets meer kon zien.

beslaan.e: From Middle Dutch beslaen. By surface analysis, be- + slaan.

s.def: to end, to finish, to conclude

beëindigen.e: From be- + eindigen, replacing earlier beënden.

s.def: to buoy off a port side by equipping it with protective barrels
a bulrush, club-rush; any of various plants from the genera Scirpus, Schoenoplectus or Bolboschoenus, particularly Schoenoplectus

bies.e: From Middle Dutch biese, from Old Dutch *biesa, from Proto-West Germanic *beusu.

s.def: to empower, to give power to

bemachtigen.e: From Middle Dutch bemachtigen. Equivalent to macht + be- -igen.

biogas.e: Perhaps borrowed. Equivalent to bio- + gas.

belonen.e: From Middle Dutch belônen. Equivalent to be- + lonen.

bedrieglijk.e: From Middle Dutch bedriegelijc. Equivalent to bedriegen + -lijk.

blasfemie.e: Borrowed from Ecclesiastical Latin blasphēmia, from Ancient Greek βλασφημία (blasphēmía, “profanity”), from βλασφημέω (blasphēméō, “to slander”).

bestemmen.e: From Middle Dutch bestemmen. Equivalent to be- + stemmen.

examples: Met veel moeite kon hij zijn woede bedwingen en rustig blijven.
De politie moest de demonstranten bedwingen om de orde te handhaven.

bedwingen.e: From Middle Dutch bedwingen. Equivalent to be- + dwingen.

examples: Om zijn gezag meer prestige te geven viel hij Kastilië binnen.

binnenvallen.e: From Middle Dutch binnenvallen. Equivalent to binnen + vallen.

bevoegd.e: Probably borrowed from German befugt. Attested since the late seventeenth century. Similar forms appear earlier in the seventeenth century, which were also likely based on the German root.

begrijpelijk.e: From Middle Dutch begripelijc. Equivalent to begrijpen + -e- + lijk.

berekenen.e: From Middle Dutch berekenen. Equivalent to be- + rekenen.

s.def: to bomb, to bombard (to attack with bombs)

examples: De tuinman heeft een nieuw bloembed aangelegd.
Er zijn veel kleurrijke bloemen in dat bloembed.

baarmoeder.e: From Middle Dutch baermoeder. Analyzable as baren (“to give birth”) + moeder (“uterus; mother”). Compare analogous German Gebärmutter.

bewerkstelligen.e: Borrowed from German bewerkstelligen. Equivalent to werkstellig + be- -en.

bedrukken.e: From Middle Dutch bedrucken. Equivalent to be- + drukken.

s.def: to paint, to put paint on, apply paint to
to prepare (particularly of food and drink)

bereiden.e: From Middle Dutch bereiden, from reiden, reden, from Old Dutch *reiden, from Proto-Germanic *raidijaną.

broos.e: From Middle Dutch broosch. Cognate with Middle Low German brôsch, Old Norse breyskr, both “brittle”. Apparently from a Proto-Germanic *brauskiz (or *brauskaz). Perhaps related with Dutch verbrijzelen, German zerbröseln, English bruise. The Dutch inflected form should be *brose, but was voiced by ...

bereid.e: From Middle Dutch bereit. Part participle of bereiden.

buur.e: From Middle Dutch buur, from Old Dutch *būr, from Proto-West Germanic *būr, from Proto-Germanic *būraz (“dweller, inhabitant”). Doublet of boer.

s.def: baboon, monkey of the genus Papio

baviaan.e:
From Middle Dutch baubijn, from Old French babuin, perhaps from baboe (“grimace”).
The spelling with -iaen (modern spelling: -iaan) first appears in the sixteenth century, becoming common in the seventeenth century, perhaps due to the Arminian controversy as a rhyme for arminiaan.

borst.e: From Middle Dutch borst, from Old Dutch brust, from Proto-West Germanic *brust, from Proto-Germanic *brusts, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰrews- (“to swell, blow, inflate”).

s.def: to look after, serve, study, promote, be watchful of [a person's interests], have [a person's interests] at heart, take to heart
to swear in, to give an oath to

beëdigen.e: From be- + eed + -igen, replacing earlier beëden.

betreden.e: From Middle Dutch betreden. Equivalent to be- + treden.

bijeen.e: From Middle Dutch biêen. Equivalent to bij + een.

s.def: to rob [with van ‘of’], to steal from
examples: Wie heeft mij beroofd?

beroven.e: From Middle Dutch berôven, from Old Dutch *birōvon, from Proto-Germanic *biraubōną; equivalent to be- + roven.

examples: De bestendige deputatie is het uitvoerend college van een Belgische provincie.
Deze verf is bestendig tegen extreme weersomstandigheden.
De bestendige vriendschap tussen hen duurde al tientallen jaren.
Deze stof is waterbestendig en brandbestendig.

botsen.e: Borrowed from German butzen, butschen, from Middle High German butzen, *bōʒen (see modern Butzen (“to beat”)) or perhaps onomatopoeic.

examples: Hij is een grote bruut.

bruut.e: From Middle Dutch bruut, from Old French brut, from Latin brūtus. Doublet of bruto.

s.def: screen (screen where an image is shown)
baker (person who bakes and sells bread, etc)
a surname originating as an occupation, meaning baker

bakker.e: From Middle Dutch backer, from Old Dutch *bakar, *baker, from Proto-Germanic *bakārijaz.

s.def: to form into a ball or similar shape; to clasp, clench (into a fist)
as plurale tantum: gutts, courage, audacity
bayonet (stabbing weapon attached on a fire-arm)
examples: De infanteristen hielden hun bajonetten klaar.

s.def: bobsleigh, bobsled, a sled used for the homonymous sport

bobslee.e: From bob + slee (“sled”), calque of English bobsleigh.

examples: Een vereiste is dat er publiek is in de bioscoop, maar liefst geen volle zaal. Te veel publiek leidt af.

s.def: a visit to the cinema; a film seen there
examples: 'Wat nou?' zegt Tom. 'Dat is heus niet alles, hoor. Ik wil het echt goed maken. Ik trakteer Sander op een bioscoopje.'

2013, Willem Brakman, Een winterreis, Querido, 5th edition (e-book) (1st edition 1961).
Wanneer Akijn zijn vader ging bezoeken, zorgde hij er meestal voorna het bezoek iets prettigs in het vooruitzicht te hebben: het bezoek aan een vriend, een wandeling of een bioscoopje.
De heer Münch heeft films doen vervaardigen, waarop teekeningen zijn gereproduceerd om met de bioscoop verschillende onderwerpen, vooral op het gebied van de wiskunde, te verduidelijken.
Hij had al veel gehoord over die bewegende toverlantaarn, die bioscoop werd genoemd en er zelfs een stuk in de krant over gelezen, maar gezien had hij het nog niet.

bioscoop.e: Borrowed from French bioscope and/or German Bioscop (now spelt Bioskop), a term used in the names of two early film projectors, developed by the Frenchman Georges Demenÿ and the German Max Skladanowsky, respectively. Compare English bioscope. Ultimately from Ancient Greek βίος (bíos, “bio-, life”...

s.def: Basque Country (a cultural region that straddles the border of Spain and France, where the Basque language is traditionally spoken)
Basque Country (an autonomous community in northern Spain)
a boy who lives in the neighbourhood
to sound, to feel in the mouth when sung or spoken
to kiss intensely, to French kiss

bekken.e: From Middle Dutch becken, from Vulgar Latin *baccinum. Doublet of bassin.

s.def: to make faces, in jest or expressively

bombardon.e: Borrowed from French bombardon, from Italian bombardone.

the place set for the end of a journey; destination

boekerij.e: From boek (“book”) + -erij (“-ery”), calque of Latin libraria, coined to replace liberij. Cognate with German Bücherei and Afrikaans boekery.

s.def: to base (to have as its foundation or starting point)

examples: Bart was een echte boerenjongen, geen inwijkeling uit de stad.

broom (utensil consisting of fibers and a long handle, used for sweeping)

bezem.e: From Middle Dutch besem, from Old Dutch *besemo, from Proto-West Germanic *besmō. Cognate with English besom, German Besen.

bassin.e: Borrowed from Middle French bassin. Doublet of bekken.

s.def: Belgian (of or pertaining to Belgium or its people)

belgisch.e: From Latin belgicus. Equivalent to Belg + -isch.

behoren.e: From Middle Dutch behôren. Equivalent to be- + horen.

examples: Zij vond een naaktkalender met brandweermannen in haar ooms privé-la.

berouw.e: From Middle Dutch berouwe. Equivalent to a deverbal from berouwen.

s.def: to (cause to) regret, to rue, to repent
examples: Snel getrouwd, lang berouwd.

berouwen.e: From Middle Dutch berouwen, from Old Dutch beriuwan, beruwan. Equivalent to be- + rouwen.

examples: Quinten besloot om te bedanken voor de aangeboden baan.

bedriegen.e: From Middle Dutch bedriegen, from Old Dutch bedriegan, from Proto-West Germanic *bidreugan.

examples: Mark is een vrolijk Haags baasje met rode koontjes en een slecht geheugen.

bezeren.e: From Middle Dutch beseren. Equivalent to zeer + be- -en.

examples: Hij baande een weg door het gras.

banen.e: From Middle Dutch banen. Equivalent to baan + -en.

s.def: a little, a tiny amount; notably in: geen barst ("not a bit")

examples: iets in bewaring geven.

s.def: initialism of Bondsrepubliek Duitsland (“Federal Republic of Germany”), FRG

buizen.e: From Middle Dutch busen, further etymology unclear.

bruidegom.e: From Middle Dutch brudegōme, from Old Dutch brūdigomo, from Proto-Germanic *brūdigumô.

berin.e: From Middle Dutch bērinne. Equivalent to beer (“bear”) + -in.

boude.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

beschadigen.e: From Middle Dutch beschadigen, replacing older beschaden. Equivalent to schade + be- -igen.

s.def: a blouse (an outer garment, usually loose, that is similar to a shirt)
examples: Ze kocht een nieuwe bloes voor haar sollicitatiegesprek.
De bloezen in die winkel zijn van goede kwaliteit.

s.def: superstition (a set of unscientific beliefs that future events may be influenced by one's behaviour in some magical or mystical way)

bijgeloof.e: From bij + geloof. Compare German Aberglauben.

examples: Ik zal nooit vrede hebben met wat voor beperking dan ook! Ik zal mij blijven pushen!
Deze zitplaats is alleen bestemd voor mensen met een beperking.

blijdschap.e: From blij + -schap. The -d- is a relic of blijde, the archaic form of blij.

s.def: to obstruct, to hinder, to hamper

belemmeren.e: From Middle Dutch belemmeren. Equivalent to belemmen + -eren. Compare German belämmert.

examples: De blaas dient wis tot berging der pis.

blaas.e: From Middle Dutch blase, blaes. A deverbal from blazen (“to blow”).

s.def: ingredient (one of the parts of a whole)

bestanddeel.e: From bestand + deel, calque of German Bestandteil.

examples: Twips twips twips. Eerst de handen op de heupen, dan de handen op de bips.

bips.e: Uncertain. Perhaps a shortening of bibbers, itself a humorous euphemism for billen (“buttocks, behind”).

barones.e: Ultimately from Middle French baronesse. Equivalent to baron + -es.

bries.e: First attested in 1596; borrowed from Middle French brise or English breeze, both from Spanish brisa.

s.def: according to, judging from, as is apparent from

beklagen.e: From Middle Dutch beclagen. Equivalent to be- + klagen.

s.def: bow (front of a boat or ship)

boeg.e: From Middle Dutch boech (“forearm”), from Old Dutch *buog, from Proto-West Germanic *bōgu, from Proto-Germanic *bōguz, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰeh₂ǵʰus (“arm”).

s.def: adverbial (of or relating to an adverb)

bankier.e: From Middle Dutch banckier, from Middle French banquier.

s.def: to judge, to try (take to court/trial)

berechten.e: From Middle Dutch berechten. Equivalent to recht + be- -en.

examples: Het succes van het project berust op de samenwerking van het hele team.

s.def: to resign oneself, to be resigned [with in ‘to’], to accept after resisting; settle for
examples: Hij lijkt te berusten in zijn lot.

berusten.e: From Middle Dutch berusten. Equivalent to be- + rusten.

bijt.e: From Middle Dutch bijt, from biten (Dutch bijten). Cognate with Middle Low German bīt, West Frisian byt.

bezetter.e: From Middle Dutch besetter. Equivalent to bezetten + -er.

s.def: to occupy oneself, to be productive
a bubble (spheric volume of air or another gas)
a bubble, (a period of) economic activity depending on speculation or embezzlement
an unknowingly socially divergent and isolated clique
a bubble (relatively isolated group of people who limited their social contacts largely to each other, e.g. to prevent infection during a pandemic)
examples:
2020 June 3, "Van vier vaste vrienden in je bubbel naar tien per week: hoe zit dat juist, en wat zijn de “zes gouden regels”?", Het Laatste Nieuws, replaced originial emphasis.
De veiligheidsafstand van 1,5 meter blijft van toepassing, behalve met huisgenoten onder eigen dak, bij kinderen jonger dan 12 jaar en mensen uit je eigen uitgebreide bubbel.
‘Het creëren van een bubbel, waarin sporters en begeleiders zijn afgeschermd van de buitenwereld, lijkt een goed concept om coronaproof te kunnen sporten.’

bubbel.e: Originally a variant of bobbel. From Middle Dutch bubbel. Several senses are semantic loans from English bubble.

plural of blad (“leaf of a tree”)

bladeren.e: From blad + -en, via the inflectional stem blader- (in fact an older plural).

examples: De bouw duurde vijf jaar.
Zij werkt in de bouw.

boenen.e: From Middle Dutch boenen, from Old Dutch *bōnon, *bōnen, from Proto-West Germanic *bōnōn, *bōnijan (“to polish”).

s.def: moo, the sound of lowing cattle

benodigd.e: From Middle Dutch benodicht (“in need, needy”), equivalent to benodigen + -t.

behalen.e: From Middle Dutch behalen. Equivalent to be- + halen.

buigzaam.e: From Middle Dutch boochsaem. Equivalent to buigen + -zaam.

s.def: to smoothen by scraping (with a plane)

beschaven.e: From Middle Dutch beschaven. Equivalent to be- + schaven. The sense “to civilize” developed from the sense of “to smoothen”, implying that one smoothens and polishes up a person. Compare French poli.

behagen.e: From Middle Dutch behagen, from Old Dutch *bihagon, from Proto-Germanic *bihagōną, from the root *hag-, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱok- (“to be able”), see also Avestan 𐬯𐬀𐬐- (sak-, “to agree”).

examples: De gemeente heeft een bestuurlijk orgaan dat verantwoordelijk is voor het nemen van beslissingen.

blindheid.e: From Middle Dutch blintheit. Equivalent to blind (“blind”) + -heid (“-ness”).

blaar.e:
From Middle Dutch blader (“vesicle”), from Old Dutch *blādara, from Proto-West Germanic *blādrā (“blister, bubble”).
Created by syncope of the -d- from Middle Dutch blader; the -d- is geminated before the -r-, similar to botter from boter and wakker from waken.

blom.e: From blom, Northern variant of bloem (“flower”).

s.def: the act of breaking or breaking in

braak.e: From Middle Dutch brâke (“fallow land”), from Proto-West Germanic *brāku. Cognate with German Brache (whence also the adjective brach), Old English brǣc (“plowed land”). Related with breken (“to break”), so called because the field is plowed (“broken”) and then left in this state.

s.def: to notice; to take notice of

bemerken.e: From Middle Dutch bemerken, from Old Dutch *bimerken, from Proto-West Germanic *bimarkijan. Equivalent to be- + merken. Cognate with German bemerken, also related to Middle Dutch and Middle High German bemarken, and English bemark.

boomgaard.e: From Middle Dutch boomgaert, from Old Dutch bōmgardo. Equivalent to boom (“tree”) + gaard (“garden”).

beschouwing.e: From Middle Dutch beschouwinge. Equivalent to beschouwen + -ing.

bezaaien.e: From Middle Dutch besayen. Equivalent to be- + zaaien.

baronie.e: From Middle Dutch baronie, from Old French baronnie, perhaps from Medieval Latin baronia. Equivalent to baron + -ie.

s.def: burrow of a badger, fox or beaver

burcht.e: From Middle Dutch borcht / burcht, alteration of earlier Middle Dutch burch / borch (surviving as modern Dutch burg), from Old Dutch burg, from Proto-West Germanic *burg, from Proto-Germanic *burgz, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰerǵʰ- (“fortified elevation”). The final -t is seemingly random, but n...

examples: nachtbraken

braken.e: From Middle Dutch braken. Equivalent to braak + -en.

beleggen.e: From Middle Dutch beleggen. Equivalent to be- + leggen.

begroeten.e: From Middle Dutch begroeten, from Old Dutch *bigruoten, from Proto-Germanic *bigrōtijaną. Equivalent to be- + groeten.

examples: De raaf had een stuk kaas in zijn bek.

examples: Hou je bek!

bek.e: From Middle Dutch bec, from Old French bec, from Latin beccus.

botter.e: Uncertain. Perhaps an action noun from bot (“flounder”) + -er after a type of fish fished for with the vessel, or from bot (“blunt”) from the characteristic shape of its bow.

bezwaar.e: From bezwaren. First attested in the sixteenth century.

belanden.e: From Middle Dutch belenden. Equivalent to be- + landen.

bult.e: From Middle Dutch bulte, from Proto-West Germanic *bulti, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰel- (“to blow, inflate, swell”). Cognate with northern German Bult, Bülte.

bespringen.e: From Middle Dutch bespringen. Equivalent to be- + springen.

bekakt.e: From bekakken. The root kak (“shit, crap”) is generally associated with poshness and pretentiousness, compare kakmadam and kouwe kak.

1731, Sesdaegsch winterbuitenleven, publ. by Abraham Ambrullaert, page 53.
't Was dien dag vry koud en 't miſtte ſtark, zo dat wy ons maer ontkleed hielden, en zo naer beneden gingen, daer de and're Heeren die zich op de Biljart of met leezen verdiverteert hadden, al gereed ſtonden om aen tafel te gaen, die ook daetelyk wiert opgediſcht.

boontje komt om zijn loontje.e: Literally, “little bean receives its wages”. From a short morality tale told in the Klucht van Oene by Jan Vos, in which a bean laughs so hard at the misfortune of its companions that its belly ruptures.

besmeren.e: From Middle Dutch besmeren, from Old Dutch *bismiren, *bismirwen, from Proto-West Germanic *bismirwijan. Equivalent to be- + smeren. Cognate with German beschmieren and English besmear. A variant Hollandic form gave rise to besmeuren.

beterschap.e: From Middle Dutch beterscap. Equivalent to beter (“better”) + -schap (“-ship, -ness”).

bespotten.e: From Middle Dutch bespotten, from Old Dutch bispotton. Equivalent to be- + spotten.

brummen.e: First attested as brimnum in 794. Etymology uncertain. Perhaps a dative plural derivation from Old Dutch brim (“edge, bank”), from Proto-Germanic *brimą. A composite derivation from brim and heem (“home, settlement”) is impossible. Compare Borssele.

bloeddiamant.e: From bloed (“blood”) + diamant (“diamant”), calque of English blood diamond.

behinderd.e: Ostensibly the past participle of long-obsolete behinderen, but synchronically to be interpreted as pseudo-participial hinder + be- -d.

examples: De busbestuurder zorgde ervoor dat alle passagiers veilig op hun bestemming aankwamen.

boekstaaf.e: From Middle Dutch boecstaef, boecstave, from Old Dutch buocstaf, *buocstavo, from Proto-Germanic *bōkastabô, *bōkastabaz (“letter, written symbol”), equivalent to boek + staaf. Compare German Buchstabe, English bookstave.

s.def: manual of confession (a list of common sins which can be consulted to examine whether one has some of them to confess)

biechtspiegel.e: From biecht (“confession”) + spiegel (“mirror”). Compare German Beichtspiegel.

braaien.e: Borrowed from Afrikaans braai (“to barbecue”), derived from Dutch braden (“to roast”).
