barnsteen.e: From barn (“burn”) + steen (“stone”). The former part of the compound is related to the Dutch verb branden (“to burn”). Cognate to English brimstone, German Bernstein (“amber”) and Icelandic brennisteinn (“sulfur, brimstone”).

s.def: a woman one is romantically involved with in addition to one's primary partner, usually in secret; a mistress; a kept woman
examples: De getatoueerde roos is eigenlijk een niemendalletje, maar het spel van Anna Magnani is van zo'n meeslepende kwaliteit, dat de rolprent er iets bijzonders door geworden is. Anna komt als Serafina tot de rampzalige bevinding dat haar overleden man een buitenvrouw heeft gehad.
Ook is het bepaald onbeschaamd als bijv. een topambtenaar elke dag een dienstauto kompleet-met-chauffeur stuurt om zijn buitenvrouw, die onderwijzeres is, naar school te brengen.

buitenvrouw.e: Compound of buiten (“outside”) + vrouw (“woman, wife”). For the sense "mistress", compare Antillean Creole fanm déwo, Haitian Creole fanm deyò.

s.def: local train, commuter train, train that calls at every stop

boemeltrein.e: From boemelen + trein, calque of German Bummelzug.

examples:
1789, Jacobus Rysdyk Takens, De hagchelijke beproeving, onbezwekene standvastigheid en godlijke uitredding, van Sadrach, Mesach en Abednego. In drie leerredenen over Daniël III, publ. by Cornelis van den Dries, page 99.
Ziet daar het heuchelijk blijeinde van het treurig en aandoenlijk ſchouwſpel, tot het welk zij door de willekeurige heerschzucht en wreedheid van een' Afgodisch' Vorst gedoemt waren!

1866, H. A. Banning, "De heks van de Amersfoorter heide", Noord en Zuid, Vol. 5, M. H. Sermon (publ.), page 629.
Doch het strookte niet met het plan des schrijvers op een blijeinde uit te lopen.

buigspier.e: From buigen (“to bend, to flex”) + spier (“muscle”).

s.def: the concept of Belgian national identity, often formulated in a self-depreciating or humorous way

belgitude.e: Borrowed from Belgian French belgitude, a neologism formed in the 70's that alludes to négritude.

examples: Nadat de bruid twee shots tequila tot zich had genomen, ging de situatie snel bergafwaarts.

to precipitate (to come out of a liquid solution into solid form)

betaalmuur.e: From betalen (“to pay”) + muur (“wall”), calque of English paywall.

examples: Zij gingen naar het restaurant met de benenwagen.

benenwagen.e: From benen (“legs”) + wagen (“car, cart”), as if the legs were a "vehicle".

bescheren.e: From Middle Dutch bescheren. Equivalent to be- + scheren (“order, arrange, prepare”).

byou.e: Modified from bijou. The digraph is fairly rare among European languages, but it is common in Dutch, and in older Dutch spellings it is generally interchangeable with . Thus, some people may think is "too Dutch" for a foreign word, and that it therefore must be a misspelling for . Compare Dyon.

brecht.e: From a common name element meaning "bright," from Proto-West Germanic *berht.

Mijn argument was soodanigh: indien het verstaen worde, dat ick was ten rechten gedeporteert, so en was ick dan niet meer een predicant ofte lidt van dat Classis ende van die vergaderinge, ende volgens en hadde ick aldaer niet een woordt te spreken; maer so het anders worde verstaen, so mocht ick mede bysitten ende spreken, als tot de vergaderinge gehoorende.

Hij vond het door Truitje gedekte hoekje met de kaas en de Haagsche beschuitjes en een kannetje karnemelk, nadat hij het licht had laten knippen, in de strakverlaten kamer; en eerst de volgende middag aan tafel, vertelde ze, daar Nico bijzat, dat die broer van juffrouw Van den Bongart er was geweest, met een felicitatie waar ze niets van begreep. 1807, Johannes le Francq van Berkhey, Natuurlyke historie van Holland, vol. 6, publ. P. H. Trap, page 162.
De derde maag … (bestaat) enkel en alleen uit bladen …, die als een boek of Psalmboek open en toeslaan. … (Zij) is de kleinste …, maar … de sterkste van vezelen en spieren, in een gedrongen, en als een dik digt gebonden boek, te regt boekpens genaamd…

boekpens.e:
Compound of boek (“book; omasum”) + pens (“stomach”). Named after the laminae (leaflets) in the omasum. Cognate with Limburgish bookmaag.
Compare German Buch, Kalender, English bible, psalterium, Norwegian bladmage, Swedish bladmage, French feuillet, Russian книжка (knižka) and Polish księgi.

bruchem.e: First attested as bruchem in the first quarter of the 13th century. Compound of broek (“marsh, marshland”) and heem (“home, settlement”).

Compound of boven (“upper, upstream”) and the dative plural form of Old Dutch lēo (“burial mound”) (cognate to Gothic 𐌷𐌻𐌰𐌹𐍅 (hlaiw, “tomb, grave”), Old Saxon hlêo (“mound, burial mound”), Old High German hlêo (“mound, burial mound”)). The element boven was...

s.def: a hamlet in West Maas en Waal, Gelderland, Netherlands

blauwesluis.e: First attested as de slusa in 1294-1295. Compound of blauw (“blue”) (derived from the hydronym Blauwe Wetering) and sluis (“lock, sluice”). Compare Blauwe Sluis, Rode Sluis, Zwartsluis and Zwartsluisje.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Wijchen, Gelderland, Netherlands

balgoij.e: First attested as balgoie in 1172. Compound derived either from Proto-Germanic *balg- (“swelling, slight elevation”) and Old Dutch ôi (“land next to water”) or Proto-Germanic *balwa- (“bad”) and *ga-agwjô- (“habitation area”).

s.def: a city and former municipality of Wijchen, Gelderland, Netherlands

batenburg.e: Potentially attested as batenburg in 1076-1081, attested with certainty as battenburg in 1166. Perhaps a compound of Old Dutch bata (“good”) and burg (“fortified settlement, castle”). A different interpretation identifies this settlement with Batavodurum.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Wijchen, Gelderland, Netherlands

bergharen.e: First attested as haren in the 12th century. Tautological compound of berg (“hill, elevated place”) and haar (“sandy ridge”). The current form of the toponym was created in a time when the original meaning of haar had been forgotten. Compare Bergerden.

s.def: a village in Meierijstad, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Wijchen, Gelderland, Netherlands

boskant.e:
* (Noord-Brabant) First attested as De Boschkant in 1868. Derived from a compound of bos (“rising forest”) and kant (“side, edge”).
* (Gelderland) First attested as Boschkant in 1899. Derived from a compound of bos (“rising forest”) and kant (“side, edge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Wijchen, Gelderland, Netherlands

bullenkamp.e: First attested as Bullenkamp in 1913. Compound of bul (“bull”) and kamp (“enclosed pasture”).

s.def: a hamlet in Heumen, Gelderland, Netherlands
a neighborhood of Nijmegen, Gelderland, Netherlands

bottendaal.e: Attested as Bottendaal in 1846. Compound of the plural form of bot (“bone”) and a stretched variant of dal (“valley, vale”). Named after a section of the city's fortifications.

s.def: a neighborhood of Nijmegen, Gelderland, Netherlands

brakkenstein.e: Attested as Mulecomshof in 1634. Likely from brak (“type of hunting dog”) + -stein (“stone building, castle”). Initially a compound of Mulecom (a family name) and hof (“court”). Originally an estate.

s.def: a neighborhood of Nijmegen, Gelderland, Netherlands

bijsterhuizen.e: Attested as Bijsterhuizen in 1822. Compound of bijster (“in an unfortunate state, impoverished”) and huizen (“houses, settlement”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Lingewaard, Gelderland, Netherlands

bemmel.e: Potentially attested as benmele in 1178, attested with certainty as in bemela in 1189. Etymology unclear. Potentially of non-Germanic origins. A proposed derivation from a compound of beemd (“pasture, field”) and lo (“light forest”) clashes with the oldest attestations.

s.def: a hamlet in Lingewaard, Gelderland, Netherlands

bergerden.e: First attested as borcheren and bargheren in 1331. Tautological compound of berg (“hill, elevated place”) and haar (“sandy ridge”). Compare Bergharen.

s.def: a hamlet in Lingewaard, Gelderland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Enkhuizen, North Holland, Netherlands

boerenhoek.e:
* (Gelderland) Attested as Boerenhoek in 1899. Compound of the plural form of boer (“farmer”) and hoek (“remote place”).
* (Noord-Holland) Compound of the plural form of boer (“farmer”) and hoek (“corner”). The neighbourhood was formerly an agricultural area.

s.def: a village in Berg en Dal, Gelderland, Netherlands

breedeweg.e: First attested as Breedeweg in 1899. Derived from the name of a road, in turn derived from a compound of breed (“broad”) and weg (“way, road”).

s.def: a hamlet in Berg en Dal, Gelderland, Netherlands

bruuk.e: First attested as Bruk in 1843. Derived from broek (“marsh, marshland”).

bankdirecteur.e: Compound of bank (“(financial) bank”) + directeur (“director, manager”).

s.def: a branch of business, as defined by specific products or services
a bell used as a signal in the stock exchange

beursagent.e: Compound of beurs (“(stock) exchange”) + agent (“agent, broker”).

s.def: Maroon or Amerindian assistant village chief; the lowest tier in the hierarchy of tribal authority
examples: “We kunnen onze mobiele telefoons niet thuis chargen en [dus] moeten we dat doen bij de polikliniek van het Mungra Medisch Centrum dat zonnepanelen heeft. Want telecommunicatie is niet meer weg te denken”, zegt Oda Banda, basya van Corneliskondre.
"Er is nog altijd verdeeldheid", bevestigt districtscommissaris Frits Dinge van bestuursressort Boven-Suriname. "De kapiteins en basya's ondersteunen granman Aboikoni niet."

s.def: enslaved person charged with overseeing work at a plantation and administering corporal punishment
examples: Overal waar ze langs kwamen was er een zwijgende groep hard aan het werk, en de basya stond aan de kant, met de zweep in de ene hand en een houwer in de andere hand.

basya.e: Unadapted borrowing from Sranan Tongo basya, from English overseer. Doublet of bastiaan and officier.

s.def: the act of art of following a twisted, curvaceous path or route
the act of twisting, digressing in one's actions, without following a single line

bochtenwerk.e: Compound of bocht (“bend, twist”) + -en- + werk (“work, act(ion)”).

s.def: a neighborhood of Doetinchem, Gelderland, Netherlands

brocade, heavy fabric interwoven with threads of precious metal

brokaat.e: From Middle Dutch brocaet, possibly via Middle French brocat, from Italian broccato, from Medieval Latin broccatus. The variants with d were influenced by Iberian languages, likely by Portuguese brocado.

s.def: a bailiwick, judicial district within which a bailiff (or bailie) has jurisdiction
the office or capacity of bailiff

baljuwschap.e: From Middle Dutch baliuschap. Equivalent to baljuw (“bailiff”) + -schap (“-ship”).

balen als een stekker.e: Literally, “to be annoyed (etc.) like a (power) plug”.

bloedgift.e: Compound of bloed (“blood”) + gift (“gift, donation; poison”).

bèta.e: Ultimately from Ancient Greek βῆτα (bêta), of Semitic origin. Doublet of beth.

collective name for several models of prefabricated single-storey wooden houses, built on storey-high concrete stilts to protect against pests and to enable passive cooling, which were manufactured and popularised by the Bruynzeel timber company between 1950 and 1990
examples: Momenteel heeft men op de meeste projecten een woning van de Bruynzeel maatschappij, waarover wij reeds bij de aankondiging schreven. Dit elegante woninkje, met drie slaapkamers[,] maakt thans een grote opgang onder de naam van Bruynzeelwoning.

Het was een warme zondagochtend in de grote droge tijd. De zon hing nog laag maar had al zoveel kracht dat het hoorbaar warm was. Buiten op het erf waren vanuit het raam de ontastbare hittetrillingen op het plaatijzeren dak van Buurvrouw d'r Bruynzeelwoning te zien.

bruynzeelwoning.e: Literally, “Bruynzeel house”; a compound of Bruynzeel (a trademark) + woning (“house, dwelling”).

balkspiraalstelsel.e: A calque of barred spiral galaxy. Equivalent to balk + spiraalstelsel.

blaasmuziek.e: From blazen (“to blow, play wind instrument”) + muziek (“music”).

blindganger.e: Compound of blind (“blind”) + ganger (“one who goes”).

beenveeg.e: From been (“leg”) + veeg (“sweep”), calque of English leg sweep.

blootshoofds.e: From Middle Dutch blootshovets. Equivalent to univerbation of bloots + hoofds, a genitive phrase.

barlage.e:
First attested as Barlege in 1851-1855. Compound of a dialectal variant of berk (“birch, Betula sp.”) and Dutch Low Saxon lage (“open space between forests”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Baarloag.

s.def: a hamlet in Mook en Middelaar, Limburg, Netherlands

bisselt.e: First attested as de Biessael in 1570. Compound toponym derived from bies (“rush”) and a second element that is either Middle Dutch sael (“unit of land”), veld (“field”) or lo (“light forest”). Compare Beesel, Hasselt and Lage Bijssel.

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen, Limburg, Netherlands

beekheuvel.e: First attested as Beekheuvel in 1913. Compound of beek (“brook, stream”) and heuvel (“hill”).

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen, Limburg, Netherlands

bergsche heide.e: First attested as Bergsche Heide in 1913. Derived from the toponym Bergen and heide (“heath, heathland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen, Limburg, Netherlands

bleijenbeek.e: First attested as blidebeke and blienbeke in the 14th century. Compound of blij (“cheerful, happy”) and beek (“brook, stream”). Originally the name of a castle.

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen, Limburg, Netherlands

bosscherheide.e: First attested as Bosscherheide in 1899. Compound of the toponym Bosch (referring to an otherwise unknown location) and heide (“heath, heathland”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Venlo, Limburg, Netherlands

belfeld.e:
First attested as belven in 1326. Etymology uncertain. The oldest attestations indicate the name is a compound formed from an uncertain first element and veld (“field”). The proposed reading of the name as a compound of Proto-Germanic *bala- (“white”) and ven (“bog, marsh”) is incorrect.
See also...

s.def: a village and former municipality of Venlo, Limburg, Netherlands

blerick.e:
First attested as Blariaco around 365. Derived from Gallo-Roman Latin Blariacum, in turn a derivation from the personal name Blarius or Blarus suffixed with -acum. Compare Bunnik, Cuijk, Geverik, Lopik, Maurik, Mook, Niftrik, Odijk, Varik, Woezik and Fellerich.
See also Limburgish Blierik.

s.def: a hamlet in Venlo, Limburg, Netherlands

bolenberg.e:
First attested as Aen den Bollenbergh in 1726. Possibly a compound of Old Dutch Bolo (a personal name) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). Compare Bolberg.
See also Limburgish Boleberg.

s.def: a hamlet in Venlo, Limburg, Netherlands

brandemolen.e:
First attested as Brandemolen in 1936. Potentially derived from brandermolen (“mill used to process malt for breweries”).
See also Limburgish Brandemeule.

s.def: a hamlet in Beesel, Limburg, Netherlands

bussereind.e:
First attested as Busserendt in 1534. Compound of the otherwise unknown toponym Bus (derived in turn from bos (“forest”)) and eind (“end, edge”).
See also Limburgish Böstering.

s.def: a village in Roermond, Limburg, Netherlands

boukoul.e:
First attested as opter buyeckulen in 1463. Compound of a first element that was at some point reinterpreted as beuk (“beech, Fagus sylvatica”) and Middle Dutch kule (“hole, pit”). No relationship with the toponymic element beukel (“small round elevation in the landscape”).
See also Limburgish De...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Roermond, Limburg, Netherlands

broekhin.e: First attested as in de Broekhinne in 1659. Compound of dialectal broek (“breeding hen”) and hin (“hen, female chicken”). Named after an inn and brewery formerly located near the current neighbourhood.

s.def: a hamlet in Echt-Susteren, Limburg, Netherlands

baakhoven.e:
First attested as beechoven in 1312 and 1358. Derived from a compound of Middle Dutch beec (“brook, stream”) and the dative plural form of hof (“farm, farmstead”). No relationship to Old Dutch Bako (a personal name).
See also Limburgish Baakhaove.

s.def: a hamlet in Echt-Susteren, Limburg, Netherlands

berkelaar.e:
First attested as berclar in the mid-11th century. Compound of berk (“birch, Betula sp.”) and laar (“intensively cultivated forest”).
See also Limburgish Berkelieër.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Sittard-Geleen, Limburg, Netherlands

broeksittard.e:
First attested as bruchsitert in 1144. Compound of broek (“marsh, marshland”) and the toponym Sittard.
See also Limburgish Broukzittert.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Sittard-Geleen, Limburg, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Beekdaelen, Limburg, Netherlands

bingelrade.e:
First attested as binghenrode in 1263. Compound of Old Dutch Bingo (a personal name) and rode (“land cleared of trees”).
See also Limburgish Bèngelder.

s.def: a hamlet in Beekdaelen, Limburg, Netherlands

breinder.e: First attested as breiderode in 1263. Compound of breed (“broad, wide”) and rode (“land cleared of trees”).

s.def: a hamlet in Beekdaelen, Limburg, Netherlands
a hamlet in Meerssen, Limburg, Netherlands

brommelen.e:
* (Beekdaelen) Derived from braam (“bramble, Rubus fruticosus”). See also Limburgish Brómmele.
* (Meerssen) Potentially attested as bromelen in 1391-1392, attested with certainty as bromelen in 1399-1400. Derived from braam (“bramble, Rubus fruticosus”). See also Limburgish Brómmele.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Brunssum, Limburg, Netherlands

bouwberg.e: First attested as Bouwberg in 1869. Potentially a compound of boede (“cottage, shack, shed”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Kerkrade, Limburg, Netherlands

bleijerheide.e:
First attested as blye and blyde in 1475. Compound of the toponym Blijde (of uncertain origin) and heide (“heath, heathland”).
See also Central Franconian Blieërhei.

s.def: a hamlet in Heerlen, Limburg, Netherlands

benzenrade.e:
First attested as benzerode and benserode in 1281. Compound of Old Dutch Benzo (a personal name) and rode (“land cleared of trees”).
See also Limburgish Benzerao.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Heerlen, Limburg, Netherlands

beersdal.e: First attested as berlesberch in 1403. Named after a nearby farmhouse.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Heerlen, Limburg, Netherlands

bekkerveld.e: Compound of the toponym Bek and veld (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Heerlen, Limburg, Netherlands

bovenste caumer.e:
First attested as kaldenborne in 1274-1294. Derived from bovenst (“upper”) and a compound of Middle Dutch kald (“cold”) and born (“spring, source”).
See also Limburgish Euvesj Kaumer, Boaves Kaumer.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Heerlen, Limburg, Netherlands
a hamlet in Simpelveld, Limburg, Netherlands

baaks-sweijer.e: Baaks is attested in 1979 as Baaks, Sweijer is first attested in 1466 as scherwier. Baaks derives from the surname Baxen and refers to a former owner of the fiefdom of Sweijer. Sweijer is potentially derived from Latin *carrucaria (“ploughable land”). Compare Swier.

s.def: a hamlet in Simpelveld, Limburg, Netherlands

baneheide.e: First attested as Baenenheidt in 1603-1604. Compound of the surname Baenen and heide (“heath, heathland”). The first element was eventually reinterpreted as baan (“thoroughfare”).

s.def: a hamlet in Simpelveld, Limburg, Netherlands

bocholtzerheide.e: First attested as Bocholtzheit in 1803-1820. Compound of the toponym Bocholtz and heide (“heath, heathland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Simpelveld, Limburg, Netherlands

bosschenhuizen.e: First attested as busgenhusen in 1378. Compound of bos (“forest”) and the plural form of huis (“house”). The reading of the first element as the surname Buskens is erroneous.

s.def: a hamlet in Simpelveld, Limburg, Netherlands

bulkemsbroek.e: Compound of the toponym Bulkem and broek (“marsh, marshland”).

s.def: a village in Valkenburg aan de Geul, Limburg, Netherlands

broekhem.e:
First attested as bruchem in 1391. Compound of broek (“marsh, marshland”) and heem (“home, settlement”).
See also Limburgish Brokem.

s.def: a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

beertsenhoven.e:
First attested as Beeretshoven in 1803-1820. Compound of Berets (a surname) and the dative plural form of hof (“farmstead, homestead”).
See also Limburgish Baetsehaove.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Oss, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

berghem.e:
* (Noord-Brabant) First attested as berchem in 1286. Compound of berg (“hill, elevated place”) and heem (“home, settlement”).
* (Limburg) First attested as berchem in 1286. Compound of berg (“hill, elevated place”) and heem (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

berghof.e:
Potentially attested as Hoevelshoeff in 1603, attested with certainty as Berghof in 1913. Compound of berg (“hill, elevated place”) and hof (“farmstead, homestead”).
See also Limburgish Berghäöf.

s.def: a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

beutenaken.e:
First attested de bottinachs in 1111. Derived from Old Dutch Boto (a personal name) or Buto and the Gallo-Roman suffix Latin -iniacas.
See also Limburgish Bäötenake.

s.def: a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

billinghuizen.e:
First attested as Billenhuizen in 1838-1857. Derived from Old Dutch Bilo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and the plural form of huis (“house”).
See also Limburgish Billinghoeze.

s.def: a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

bommerig.e:
First attested as Bamberg in 1536. Potentially derived from a compound of ban (“legal district, legal boundary”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). Compare Banholt.
See also Limburgish Bómmerig.

s.def: a village in Eijsden-Margraten, Limburg, Netherlands

banholt.e:
First attested as bannoyt in 1294-1295. Compound of ban (“legal district, legal boundary”) and hout (“rising deciduous forest”). Compare Bommerig.
See also Limburgish Tebannet.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Eijsden-Margraten, Limburg, Netherlands

bemelen.e:
First attested as bemele in 1096. Etymology unknown. Potentially a compound of an uncertain first element and lo (“light forest”). A derivation from Old Dutch male (“place where courts are held”) is unlikely.
See also Limburgish Bieëmele.

s.def: a hamlet in Eijsden-Margraten, Limburg, Netherlands

bergenhuizen.e:
First attested as Bergershoysen in 1537. Compound of either an otherwise unknown toponym Berg (derived from berg (“hill, elevated place”)) or Bergens (a surname) and the dative plural form of huis (“home”).
See also Limburgish Bergenhoëze.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Eijsden-Margraten, Limburg, Netherlands

breust.e: First attested as villam Brusti in 965. Perhaps derived from Old Dutch brus (“thorn bush, brushwood”) with collectivising suffix -t or related to Old High German brust (“abyss, steep incline”). Compare Brust.

s.def: a hamlet in Eijsden-Margraten, Limburg, Netherlands

bruisterbosch.e:
First attested as terra nova de sarto in 1157. Compound of the toponym Breust and bos (“rising forest”).
See also Limburgish Bruusjterbusj.

s.def: building block (component that is part of a larger system)

bouwsteen.e: From bouwen + steen. Compare German Baustein.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

boschstraatkwartier.e:
Derived from a compound of the toponym Boschstraat and kwartier (“quarter”). Named after the road connecting Maastricht to 's-Hertogenbosch.
See also Limburgish Boschstroatkerteer.

a neighbourhood of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

biesland.e:
* (Zuid-Holland) Attested as De Bieslants Polder in 1611. Compound of bies (“club-rush, Scirpus sp.”) and land (“land”). Named after a former fiefdom.
* (Limburg) Attested as Bies land in 1838-1857. Compound of bies (“club-rush, Scirpus sp.”) and land (“land”). Formerly an independent hamlet.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

brusselsepoort.e: First attested as Porta Bruxellensis around 1580. Compound of the toponym Brussel and poort (“gate”). Named after one of the former gates of the city.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

boschpoort.e: First attested as Hoochter port in 1581. Compound of the toponym 's-Hertogenbosch and poort. Named after one of the former gates of the city which was initially named after the Hoichte monastery.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

bosscherveld.e: Compound of the toponym 's-Hertogenbosch and veld (“field”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

belvédère.e: First attested as Belvedère (sic) in 1869. Derived from French belvédère (“belvedere”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

beatrixhaven.e: Derived from a compound of Beatrix (a personal name) and haven (“harbour”). Named after Princess Beatrix of the Netherlands.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

borgharen.e: Potentially attested as curtim aput haren in 1170-1180, attested with certainty as apud haram in 1180 and 1182. broekhoven.e:

s.def: a village in Stein, Limburg, Netherlands

berg aan de maas.e:
First attested as berge in 1294-1295. Derived from berg (“hill, elevated place”). The identifier aan de Maas ("on the Meuse") was added at a later date to distinguish the settlement from nearby villages also called Berg.
See also Limburgish Berg.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Maasgouw, Limburg, Netherlands

beegden.e: Potentially attested as beegedem in 1202, attested with certainty as bekede in 1264. Etymology uncertain. brachterbeek.e:
First attested as beke in 1427. Compound derived from a hydronym and beek (“brook, stream”).

bilt.e: First attested as het Bild in 1867. Perhaps derived from Limburgish bild (“image of a saint”) after a local chapel. No relation to Middle Dutch *belt (“belt, border zone”) or Het Bildt.

baexem.e:

buggenum.e:

babyloniënbroek.e: First attested as babilonia in 1131. Compound of the toponym Babylon and broek (“marsh, marshland”). The settlement began as a land-reclamation venture by the Sint Truiden monastery. The village was named after Babylon either because of its remote location or (according to local legend) because o...

s.def: a hamlet in Altena, North Brabant, Netherlands

biesheuvel.e: First attested as BIESHEUVEL of Biesseheuvel in 1840. Compound of bies (“rush, Scirpus sp.”) and heuvel (“hill”).

s.def: a hamlet in Drimmelen, North Brabant, Netherlands

binnen-moerdijk.e: First attested as De Binnen Moerdijk in 1838-1857. Compound of binnen (“within”) and the toponym Moerdijk. So named because the settlement is located behind the levee which Moerdijk derives its name from.

s.def: a hamlet in Drimmelen, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Steenbergen, North Brabant, Netherlands

blauwe sluis.e:
* (Drimmelen) First attested as De Blauwe Sluis in 1838-1857. Derived from blauw (“blue”) and sluis (“lock, sluice”). Compare Blauwesluis, Rode Sluis, Zwartsluis and Zwartsluisje.
* (Steenbergen) First attested as bij de Blauwe Sluijs in 1573-1576. Derived from blauw (“blue”) and sluis (“lock, sl...

s.def: a hamlet in Gilze en Rijen, North Brabant, Netherlands

biestraat.e: First attested as biestraten in 1297. Derived from a dialectal variant of bij (“bee, Apis sp.”) and straat (“street, road”).

s.def: a hamlet in Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Gilze en Rijen, North Brabant, Netherlands

bolberg.e:
* (Breda) First attested as opten Bolberch in 1513. Compound of bol (“round”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). Compare Bolenberg.
* (Gilze en Rijen) First attested as opten Bolberch in 1545. Compound of bol (“round”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). Compare Bolenberg.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Waalwijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

baardwijk.e: First attested as barduvich in 1136. Compound of Old Dutch barda (“edge, end”) and wijk (“village, settlement”). Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Waalwijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

besoijen.e: First attested as bizoyen in 1312-1350. Perhaps derived from a compound of Middle Dutch bys (“rush, Scirpus sp.”) and ooi (“land next to a watercourse”). Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Tilburg, North Brabant, Netherlands

berkel-enschot.e: Berkel is first attested as bercle in 1214. Enschot is first attested as endeschit in 1164. Berkel derives from a compound of berk (“birch, Betula sp.”) and lo (“light forest”). Enschot derives from a compound of eind (“end, edge”) and Old Dutch *schid (“boundary, watershed”). Formerly two indepe...

s.def: a village in Tilburg, North Brabant, Netherlands

biezenmortel.e: First attested as den byesmortel by ryetdonck in 1487. Compound of bies (“rush, Scirpus sp.”) and mortel (“low-lying swampy place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Tilburg, North Brabant, Netherlands

berkeind.e: First attested as Het Berkeind in 1838-1857. Compound of berk (“birch, Betula sp.”) and eind (“end, edge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Goirle, North Brabant, Netherlands

bakertand.e: First attested as dat bakartlant in 1404. Compound of an unclear first element and land (“land, area”). The first element has been interpreted as a personal name Middle Dutch *Bakart or as a sub-compound derived from an unclear element and Middle Dutch aert (“farmland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Goirle, North Brabant, Netherlands

boschkens.e: Attested as Boschkers [sic] in 1936. Derived from the plural form of a diminutive form of bos (“rising forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Goirle, North Brabant, Netherlands

breehees.e: First attested as Breehees in 1838-1857. Compound of breed (“broad, wide”) and a reflex of Old Dutch *hēsi (“brushwood”), a common toponymic element.

s.def: a village and municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands

baarle-nassau.e: Potentially attested as barle in 1237, attested with certainty as barle in 1260. Compound of a toponym derived from Middle Dutch baer (“barren, infertile”) and lo (“light forest”) suffixed with Nassau. The element Nassau was added to distinguish the settlement from Baarle-Hertog. Compare Baal, Ba...

s.def: a hamlet in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

balleman.e: First attested as baleman in 1490. Derived from Baleman (a surname).

s.def: a hamlet in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

boslust.e: Derived from boslust (“woodland fun”). Likely derived from the name of a house.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

bavel.e: First attested as bavele in 1299. Potentially a compound of Old Dutch Bavo (a personal name) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

belcrum.e: First attested as op belckenem in 1377. Compound of an unclear first element and heem (“home, settlement”). The first element may be an otherwise unknown toponym *Belk, which appears to be derived from blok (“piece of farmland ringed by a ditch or fence”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

biesdonk.e: First attested as in den biesdonc in 1393. Compound of bies (“rush, Scirpus sp.”) and donk (“sandy hill protruding above a swamp”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

blauwe kei.e: First attested as den blauwen kaey in 1699. Derived from blauw (“blue”) and kei (“boulder, rock”). Named after a rock which was used as a boundary marker.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

boeimeer.e: First attested as boymere in 1295. Compound of either dialectal boei (“mud, sludge”) or Middle Dutch Boy (a personal name) and meer (“lake, pool”).

s.def: a hamlet in Etten-Leur, North Brabant, Netherlands

bankenstraat.e: First attested as De Banken in 1866. Compound of the plural form of bank (“area of peat bog that has been separated into parallel strips of land”) and straat (“street, road”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zundert, North Brabant, Netherlands

breedschot.e: First attested as int breescot in 1414. Compound of breed (“broad, wide”) and schot (“enclosed space, fenced-in land”).

s.def: a hamlet in Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands

boeiink.e: First attested as boedonc and bodonc in 1359. Likely a compound of Middle Dutch boede (“cottage, shack, shed”) and donk (“sandy hill protruding above a swamp”). Compare Boeimeer.

s.def: a hamlet in Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands

borteldonk.e: First attested as bortendonc in 1327. Compound of an unclear first element and donk (“sandy hill protruding above a swamp”). The first element may be cognate to Middle Dutch bortelen (“bubble up”).

s.def: a hamlet in Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands

brembosch.e: Potentially attested as brembosch in 1292, attested with certainty as brambosch in 1753. Compound of brem (“broom, Cytisus scoparius”) or braam (“raspberry, Rubus sp.”) and bos (“rising forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands

bulkenaar.e: First attested as borkelaer in 1359. Compound of Middle Dutch bork (“type of birch”) and laar (“intensively cultivated forest”). Compare Borculo and Borkel en Schaft.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands

borchwerf.e: First attested as de borchwerff in 1473. Compound of burg (“reinforced settlement, castle”) and werf (“artifical hill used for habitation”). Originally the name of a field.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands

burgerhout.e: First attested as Burgerhout in 1786. Compound of borg (“castle”) and hout (“rising deciduous forest”).

s.def: a village in Halderberge, North Brabant, Netherlands

bosschenhoofd.e: First attested as Bossenhooft in 1740. Compound of the toponym Oudenbosch and hoofd (“end of a canal in a turf extraction area”).

s.def: a hamlet in Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

barlaque.e: First attested as berlake in 1278. Derived from a hydronym which is probably derived from a compound of Middle Dutch baer (“barren, infertile”) and laak (“natural watercourse, channel in a turf extraction area”). Compare Attelaken.

s.def: a hamlet in Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

bovensluis.e: First attested as de Bovensluis in 1849. Derived from bovensluis (“upstream lock”).

s.def: a hamlet in Steenbergen, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Steenbergen, North Brabant, Netherlands

boompjesdijk.e: First attested as Boom of Drogen Dijk in 1856-1857. Compound of a diminutive plural form of boom (“tree”) and droge dijk (“inland levee, levee not touching a body of water”). The first element was originally the name of a field.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands

bergse plaat.e: Derived from the toponym Bergen op Zoom and plaat (“sand bar visible at low tide”).

s.def: to grow upon, to cover with growths
any fish (or other creatures) that are not targeted as a catch but are unintentionally caught, and often discarded back into the sea; bycatch
to span over, to span across
to cover an object with something that is stretched (wires, strings, cloth etc.)
to affect or spoil with seawater

bewrijven.e: From Middle Dutch bewriven. Equivalent to be- + wrijven (“to rub”).

s.def: to grind, to remove the rough edges by grinding

bemeten.e: From Middle Dutch bemeten, from Old Dutch *bemetan, from Proto-West Germanic *bimetan. Equivalent to be- + meten. Cognate with German bemessen and obsolete English bemete.

besteken.e: From Middle Dutch bestēken. Equivalent to be- + steken.

s.def: to piss at, to piss on

bezeiken.e: From Middle Dutch beseiken. Equivalent to be- + zeiken (“to piss”).

bespuwen.e: From Middle Dutch bespiewen, bespuwen. Equivalent to be- + spuwen (“to spit”).

s.def: to strive, to attempt by labouring

bearbeiden.e: From Middle Dutch bearbeiden. Equivalent to be- + arbeiden (“to labour”).

s.def: to commit to the earth, bury

beaarden.e: From be- + aarden (“to bury”) or aarde (“earth”) + be- -en.

s.def: to make anxious, to make fearful, to alarm
to cool down, to wane, to become less intense

bekoelen.e: From Middle Dutch becoelen. Equivalent to be- + koelen (“to cool”).

s.def: a hamlet in Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands

botshoofd.e:
Attested as Botshooft in 1617. Potentially a compound of bots (“dead on, right on”) and hoofd (“pier, jetty”).
See also Zealandic Pesoôt.

s.def: to ornate, to cover with flowers
to flour, to cover with flour
to inherit, to acquire through inheritance
a village and former municipality of Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

bruinisse.e:
First attested as brunesse in 1480-1481. Compound of Middle Dutch bru (“sludge, dreck”) or bruy and Middle Dutch nisse (“headland, spit”). Compare Breudijk and Brouwhuis.
See also Zealandic Bru.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

brouwershaven.e:
First attested as brijdorpersluis around 1305. Likely a compound of Brouwer (a surname) and haven (“harbour”). Compare Biervliet.
See also Zealandic Brouwes'aeven, Brouw.

s.def: a village in Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

burgh-haamstede.e: Burgh is first attested as burgt in 1219. Haamstede is first attested as haemstede in 1229. Burgh is derived from Middle Dutch burg (“fortified settlement, castle”). The settlement sprang up around a castle intended to secure the area against Viking raids. Haamstede is derived from Middle Dutch h...

s.def: a former village and former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

burgh.e: Named after Burgh, currently part of Burgh-Haamstede.

s.def: a hamlet in Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

beldert.e: First attested as bellaerde in 1470-1471. Derived from Proto-Indo-European *bʰel- (“swollen, bloated”).

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

brijdorpe.e:
First attested as bridorpia in 1231. Compound of Middle Dutch bri (“sludge, unstable ground”) and the dative singular form of dorp (“village, settlement”).
See also Zealandic Briepe.

s.def: a hamlet in Schouwen-Duiveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

burghsluis.e:
Attested as Burgtsluis around 1750. Compound of the toponym Burgh and sluis (“lock, sluice”).
See also Zealandic Burgsluus.

s.def: a former hamlet and former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

bloois.e: Named after the van Bloois family, the former lords of the settlement and fiefdom.

s.def: a former village and former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

bommenede.e: First attested as insula bomne in 1165. Compound of an unclear first element and a variant of Middle Dutch aa (“body of water, river”). The first element may be cognate to Middle Dutch bomme (“small barrel”), Limburgish bommel (“tin canteen”).

s.def: to drip on, to drip at
a village and former municipality of Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

biggekerke.e:
First attested as bigghenkerka in 1231. Compound of Old Dutch Biggo (a personal name) (a familiar version of Bighard or Bigwald) and kerk (“church”).
See also Zealandic Bîkerke, Béékerke.

s.def: a hamlet in Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

boudewijnskerke.e:
First attested as Ecclesiam Baldwini in 1235. Compound of Middle Dutch Baldwin (a personal name) and kerk (“church”).
See also Zealandic Buiskerke.

s.def: a hamlet in Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

buttinge.e:
First attested as buttinghe in 1249. Derived from Old Dutch bot (“tree stump, tree trunk”) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-.
See also Zealandic Ter Bottienge.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

buiten de veste.e: Derived from buiten (“outside of”) and veste (“fortress, fortifications”).

s.def: a former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

buttinge en zandvoort.e: Named after Buttinge and the former fiefdom of Zandvoort.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Vlissingen, Zeeland, Netherlands

baskensburg.e: First attested as Baskensburgh in 1655. Named after a former estate.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Middelburg, Zeeland, Netherlands

brigdamme.e:
First attested as brigdamme around 1205. Compound of an umlauted form of brug (“bridge, jetty”) and the dative singular form of dam (“dam”).
See also Zealandic Perdamme.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

baarland.e:
First attested as baeland in 1295. Compound of Middle Dutch baer (“barren, infertile”) and land (“land”).
See also Zealandic Baerland.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

borssele.e:
First attested as brumsale in 976. Compound of Old Dutch *brum (“edge, bank”) and sale (“hall home”). Compare Brummen.
See also Zealandic Bossele.

s.def: a hamlet in Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

baarsdorp.e:
First attested as barsdorp in 1278. Compound of Old Dutch Baer (a personal name) and dorp (“farmstead, village”).
See also Zealandic Baesdurp.

s.def: a hamlet in Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

bakendorp.e:
First attested as badekendorp in 1285-1286. Compound of Middle Dutch Badeke (a personal name) and dorp (“farmstead, village”).
See also Zealandic Bikkedurp.

s.def: a hamlet in Goes, Zeeland, Netherlands

blauwewijk.e:
Attested as Blauwe-Wijk in 1936. Compound of blauw (“blue”) and wijk (“settlement, neighbourhood”). Named for the colour of the roof tiles of the settlement's first houses. The element blauw was added to distinguish the settlement from Roodewijk.
See also Zealandic Blaeuwewiek.

s.def: a village in Kapelle, Zeeland, Netherlands

biezelinge.e:
First attested as byzelinge in 1246. Potentially derived from Old Dutch Bisilo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-. An alternative hypothesis proposes a derivation from an ancient hydronym.
See also Zealandic Biezelehe.

s.def: a former village and former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

boschkapelle.e:
Compound of bos (“forest”) and kapel (“chapel”).
The village was gradually absorbed into Vogelwaarde.

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

baalhoek.e: Attested as Balhode in 1665. Likely a compound of the toponym Balle (a former village) and hoek (“corner”).

s.def: a city and former municipality of Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

biervliet.e: First attested as birulit in 1183. Originally a hydronym. The hydronym is a compound of Proto-Germanic *birnu- (“manure, dreck”) or bier (“beer”) and vliet (“running watercourse”). Compare Brouwershaven.

s.def: a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

bouchauterhaven.e: Attested as Bouchhautse haven around 1750. Compound of the toponym Boekhoute and haven (“harbour”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

breskens.e: First attested as te brescins around 1300. Etymology uncertain. A proposed derivation from Middle Dutch britse (“latticework”) does not match the oldest attestations. Potentially derived from bres (“hole, opening”) or Zealandic bres (“chunk of dune sand loosened by the sea”), here referring to a ...

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

bakkersdam.e: Attested as Bakkersdam in 1838-1857. Compound of Bakker (a surname) and dam (“dam”).

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

balhofstede.e: Attested as Balhofstee in 1838-1857. Compound of either bal (“bullet, cannonball”) or Middle Dutch baelje (“fencing, pallisade”) and hofstede (“homestead, farmstead”).

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

boerenhol.e: Attested as Boerenhol in 1838-1857. Potentially derived from the plural form of boer (“farmer”) and hol (“lair, hole”). Although the second element is often interpreted as Zealandic nol (“remnant of a levee”), there is no indication going by the oldest attestations that the toponym actually conta...

s.def: a hamlet in Utrechtse Heuvelrug, Utrecht, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Lelystad, Flevoland, Netherlands

boswijk.e:
* (Utrecht) First attested as Mijn Genoegen in 1820. Compound of bos (“forest”) and wijk (“place, settlement”). Named after a former estate.
* (Flevoland) Compound of bos (“forest”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's sub-neighbourhoods are all named after various types of fo...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Lelystad, Flevoland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

bouwmeesterbuurt.e: Compound of bouwmeester (“master builder”) and buurt (“neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after various architects and builders.

a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

a neighbourhood of Edam-Volendam, North Holland, Netherlands

bloemenbuurt.e: All derived from a compound of the plural form of bloem (“flower”) and buurt (“neighbourhood”). The neighbourhoods' streets are named after various flowers.

s.def: a village in Dronten, Flevoland, Netherlands

biddinghuizen.e: Attested as Biddinghuizen (vroeger Bremerberg genoemd) in 1959. Named after a former settlement, the name of which is first attested in 793 and which derives from Old Dutch *Bidin (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and the plural form of huis (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Kampen, Overijssel, Netherlands

bisschopswetering.e: First attested as van des bisscopsweteringe in 1426. Compound of bisschop (“bishop”) and wetering (“drainage channel”). The element bisschop refers to Jan Van Arkel (ca. 1314-1378), the Prince-Bishop of Utrecht who initiated the construction of the channel.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Kampen, Overijssel, Netherlands

bruneppe.e: First attested as bronepe in 1329. Compound of bron (“source, spring”) and Old Dutch *apa (“river, watercourse”). Formerly an independent hamlet.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Kampen, Overijssel, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

blokzijl.e: First attested as groote zijl in 1375. Compound of blok (“beam”) and zijl (“discharge sluice”).

s.def: a village in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

belt-schutsloot.e: First attested as Sandbeld en Schutsloot in 1748. Compound of dialectal belt (“slightly elevated place”) and the hydronym Schutsloot, which is derived from schutsloot (“ditch with a dam”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

blankenham.e: First attested as in den hamme around 1420. Named after German Blankenheim. The name was given by the then-Prince-Bishop of Utrecht, Frederick of Blankenheim (around 1355-1423).

s.def: a hamlet in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

barsbeek.e: First attested as berspijck in 1363. Compound of Middle Dutch beer (“manure, mud which looks like manure”) and spijk (“headland, spit”). Compare Waspik.

s.def: a hamlet in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

basserveld.e: Compound of the toponym Basse and veld (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

blauwe hand.e: First attested as Buitenkluft in 1764. Derived from blauw (“blue”) and hand (“hand”). Named after a former inn which used a blue hand on its signage.

s.def: a former municipality of Overijssel, Netherlands

brederwiede.e: Compound of breed (“broad”) and dialectal wiede (“pool”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

berkum.e: Potentially attested as barkmen in 1233, attested with certainty as bercmede in 1364. Compound of Middle Dutch berke (“birch, Betula sp.”) and an unknown second element. Compare Bathmen, Besthmen, Diermen, Fortmond, Giethmen, Helpman, Hertme, Ratum, Reutum, Thamen, Wachtum and Wijthmen. Originall...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

brinkhoek.e:
First attested as Berkum Brinkhoek in 1867. Compound of brink (“pasture, village green”) and hoek (“corner, isolated place”). Formerly an independent hamlet.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Brinkers-oeke.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

bruggenhoek.e:
Compound of brug (“bridge”) and hoek (“corner, isolated place”). Formerly an independent hamlet.
See also Dutch Low Saxon bruggen-oeke.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

bollebieste.e: Derived from Dutch Low Saxon bollebieste (“bull”). Named after a former bull market.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

bagijneweide.e:
Compound of begijn (“Beguine”) and weide (“pasture”). Named after a former Beguine complex.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Bagienewei.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands
a hamlet in Ommen, Overijssel, Netherlands

beerze.e: First attested as beerse in 1328. Either derived from a Germanic term cognate to or directly from Latin bersa (“enclosed hunting grounds”). Compare Bears, Beers, Beersdonk, Besselaar, Middelbeers, Oostelbeers and Westelbeers.

s.def: a hamlet in Ommen, Overijssel, Netherlands

beerzerveld.e: First attested as Beerzerveld around 1860. Compound of the toponym Beerze and veld (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ommen, Overijssel, Netherlands

besthmen.e: First attested as bestmannyng in 1381-1383. Etymology unknown. Not a compound of best (“best”) and man (“man”). Compare Bathmen, Berkum, Diermen, Fortmond, Giethmen, Helpman, Hertme, Ratum, Reutum, Thamen, Wachtum, Wijthmen.

s.def: a village in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

balkbrug.e: First attested as Balkbrug in 1812. Derived from balkbrug (“beam bridge”). Named after a nearby bridge.

s.def: a village in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

bergentheim.e: First attested as bergenthem and berchenthem in 1381-1383. Compound of a first element derived from Proto-Germanic *burgundjô- (“elevated location”) and heem (“home, settlement”). Compare Borne and Bornholm.

s.def: a village in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

bruchterveld.e: First attested as Bruchter Veld in 1866. Compound of the toponym Brucht and veld (“field”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

baalder.e: First attested as bodelar around 1300. Compound of Old Dutch Bodo (a personal name) and laar (“intensively cultivated forest”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

baalderveld.e: Compound of the toponym Baalder and veld (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

benedenvaart.e: Compound of beneden (“down, lower”) and vaart (“canal”). The settlement was named for its location downstream from Dedemsvaart.

s.def: a hamlet in Stadskanaal, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

braamberg.e:
* (Groningen) First attested as Braamberg in 1781. Compound of braam (“raspberry, Rubus sp.”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). See also Dutch Low Saxon Broambaarg.
* (Overijssel) First attested as Braemberch in 1665. Compound of braam (“raspberry, Rubus sp.”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). ...

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

brucht.e: First attested as broch and brucht in 1381-1383. Potentially derived from Middle Dutch broec (“marsh, marshland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Twenterand, Overijssel, Netherlands

bruinehaar.e: First attested as Bruine Haar in 1748. Compound of bruin (“brown”) and haar (“sandy ridge”). Compare Witharen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Twenterand, Overijssel, Netherlands
a village in Raalte, Overijssel, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Harderwijk, Gelderland, Netherlands

broekland.e:
* (Overijssel) Attested as Broekland in 1803. Derived from broekland (“marshland”). See also Dutch Low Saxon Broeklaand.

First attested as Bikkershorn in 1899. Compound of Bikker (a surname) and horn (“corner”).

s.def: a hamlet in Oldambt, Groningen, Netherlands

booneschans.e:
First attested as Booner Schans in the end of the 16th century. Compound of Booner (a surname) and schans (“sconce”). Named after a sconce constructed by Albert Booner in 1589.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Bonerschaanze.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

bierum.e:
First attested as berum in the 13th century. Derived from the dative plural form of Old Frisian bêre (“barn, house”). Compare Burum, Hogebieren, Pietersbierum and Sexbierum.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Baaierm.

s.def: a village in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

borgsweer.e:
First attested as borgisweer in 1432. Compound of Old Frisian burch (“fortified settlement, castle, levee”) and werve (“artificial mound used for habitation”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Börgsweer.

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

biessum.e:
First attested as bisashem in the 10th or 11th century. Compound of Old Frisian Bisi (a personal name) and heem (“home, settlement”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Biezum.

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

baamsum.e: First attested as bompsum in 1454. Likely a compound of Old Frisian *Bame (a personal name) and heem (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

binnen ae.e: First attested as AE (BINNEN) in 1839. Derived from the hydronym Binnen Aa, in turn derived from binnen (“inner”) and aa (“watercourse, river”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

baflo.e:
First attested as bestlon [read: beftlon] in 944. Compound of Old Frisian befta (“back, behind”) and the dative plural form of lo (“light forest, pool”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Bavvelt.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

bedum.e:
First attested as bédorouualda in the 10th or 11th century. Derived from the dative plural form of Old Frisian bed (“elevated terrain”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Beem.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

barnegaten.e: First attested as Barnjegat in 1781. Compound of a dialectal form of branden (“burn”) and the plural form of gat (“kolk lake, hole in a levee”). The toponym refers to two restored levees along the Reitdiep.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

bellingeweer.e:
First attested as beltkeweer in 1462. Compound of Old Frisian Belle (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and weer (“artificial mound used for habitation”). Formerly an independent hamlet.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Bellenweer.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

beusum.e: First attested as Beusma and Beusums in 1525. Derived from West Frisian Beusma (a surname). Originally the name of a fortified manor.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

bokum.e:
First attested as Bokum in 1781. Potentially derived from West Frisian Bokema (a surname). Originally the name of a farmhouse.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Bökkum.

s.def: a village in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

boerakker.e: First attested as de Buir Acker in 1781. Compound of boer (“farmer”) and akker (“farmland”). Originally the name of a field.

s.def: a village in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

briltil.e: First attested as den Bril in 1632. Compound of a variant of briel (“soggy overgrown pasture”) and til (“fixed bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

bakkerom.e: First attested as Bakkrum in 1913. Etymology unknown. Potentially derived from a compound of a personal name and oom (“uncle”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

balmahuizen.e:
First attested as nae ballema gelegen in 1571. Compound of West Frisian Ballema (a surname) and the plural form of huis (“house”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Baalmhoezen.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

barnwerd.e:
First attested as tho berwert in 1526. Compound of Old Frisian bar (“barren, bald”) and wert (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Baarnwerd.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

beswerd.e:
First attested as in buxingi in 855. Compound of Old Frisian *Bokse (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and wert (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Beeswerd.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Soest, Utrecht, Netherlands

boerenstreek.e:
* (Groningen) First attested as de Boerestreek in 1851. Compound of boer (“farmer”) and streek (“row of houses”).
* (Utrecht) Compound of the plural form of boer (“farmer”) and streek (“line, path”). Originally the name of a footpath.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

bokkebuurt.e: Compound of Dutch Low Saxon bok (“goat”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, hamlet”). The toponym derives from the fact that the hamlet's inhabitants kept goats instead of cows due to their limited means.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

bolshuizen.e:
First attested as Bolshuizen in 1851-1855. Compound of Bols (a surname) or Bolsema (a surname) and the plural form of huis (“house”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Bolshoezen.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

breemen.e: First attested as Breemhaer in 1508. Derived from brem (“broom, Cytisus scoparius”). Not derived from the toponym Bremen.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

brillerij.e: First attested as Brillerij in 1851-1855. Likely derived from Bril (a surname) suffixed with -erij. Compare Heuvelderij, Schimmelarij, Schutterij and Stadterij.

a former municipality of Friesland, Netherlands

baarderadeel.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Baarderadiel, a compound of the toponym Baerd and diel (“district, rural administrative area”).

barradeel.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Barradiel, a compound of the toponyms Seisbierum and diel (“district, rural administrative area”). The municipality was named after an older form of the toponym Sexbierum (Sixtibarra).

s.def: a former municipality of Friesland, Netherlands

boornsterhem.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Boarnsterhim, a compound of the hydronym De Boarn and him (“polder, land located in a riverbend, land next to water”).

s.def: a village in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

boelenslaan.e: First attested as Boelenslaan in 1899. Compound of Boelens (a surname) and laan (“lane”). Named after the road which the village sprang up around.

s.def: a hamlet in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

* (Súdwest-Fryslân) First attested as t Blauw Huys in 1718. Calque of West Frisian Blauhûs, a compound of blau (“blue”) and hûs (“house”). Named after a water board tax office with conspicuous blue roof tiles; these were a rare sight due to their high cost. Compare Roodhuis.
* (Achtkarspelen) Cal...

blauwverlaat.e: First attested as Blaauw Verlaat in 1844. Compound of blauw (“blue”) and verlaat (“culvert”). Named after a nearby culvert.

s.def: a hamlet in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

buwetille.e: Calque of West Frisian Bouwetille, a compound of Buwe (a personal name) and tille (“fixed bridge”). Named after landowner Buwe Harkema.

s.def: a hamlet in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

buweklooster.e: First attested as conventus buonis in 1408. Calque of West Frisian Bouwekleaster, a compound of Buwe (a personal name) and kleaster (“monastery”). Named after a former monastery founded by landowner Buwe Harkema.

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

birdaard.e: Potentially attested as breitenfurt in 944, attested with certainty as berdawird in 1417. Borrowed from West Frisian Burdaard, derived in turn from a compound of either Old Frisian bird (“shore, edge”) or brēd (“broad, wide”) and uurð (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

blija.e: First attested as blitha in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Blije, derived in turn from a hydronym cognate to English blithe or, alternatively, from a compound of Old Frisian blij (“silt”) and hem (“village”). Compare Blijham.

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

bornwird.e: Potentially attested as bonfurt and bonewirt in 825-842, attested with certainty as brunnenuurt in 944. Borrowed from West Frisian Boarnwert, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian brun (“source, small watercourse”) and uurt (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

brantgum.e: First attested as brontegum in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Brantgum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Brant (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hem (“home, settlement”). Compare Mantgum and Tjeintgum.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

beintemahuis.e: First attested as Banthiema Huys in 1543. Calque of West Frisian Beintemahûs, a compound of Beintema (a surname) and hûs (“house”). Named after a manor.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

betterwird.e: First attested as betterwird in 1387. Calque of West Frisian Betterwurd, derived in turn from a compound of a personal name or Old Frisian betera (“better”) and wirdt (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

bollingawier.e: Potentially attested as orlinguerba around 825-842, attested with certainty as bollingaweer in 1511. s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

bonte hond.e: Derived from bont (“bruised”) and hond (“dog”), as an allusion to the proverb als de bonte hond bekend staan. Originally the name of an inn.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

bornwirdhuizen.e: First attested as Bornwerderhuijsen in 1511. Calque of West Frisian Boarnwerthuzen, a compound of the toponym Boarnwert and the plural form of hûs (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

boteburen.e: First attested as Boteburen in 1853. Calque of West Frisian Boatebuorren, a compound of Bote (a personal name) or Boate and the plural form of buer (“home, cottage”). Potentially named after a former inhabitant.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

broeksterwoude.e: First attested as De Broek in 1718. Calque of West Frisian Broeksterwâld, a compound of the toponym Broek and wâld (“marsh forest, swamp forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Tytsjerksteradiel, Friesland, Netherlands

bartlehiem.e: First attested as Bethlehem in 1232. Derived from Bethlehem, the name of a former Premonstratensian nunnery. The toponym was gradually reinterpreted to a compound of West Frisian Bartle (a personal name) and hiem (“home”).

s.def: a village in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

britsum.e: First attested as bruggiheim in 944. Borrowed from West Frisian Britsum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian bregge (“bridge”) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

baardburen.e: First attested as brauwert in 1435. Calque of West Frisian Baarderbuorren, a compound of the toponym Brauwert (derived in turn from Old Frisian brâda (“broad”) and wert (“artificial mound used for habitation”)) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a village in Smallingerland, Friesland, Netherlands

boornbergum.e: First attested as Berghum and Bergum in 1543. Borrowed from West Frisian Boarnburgum, derived in turn from a compound of the hydronym Boarn and the dative plural form of Old Frisian berch (“hill, elevated place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Smallingerland, Friesland, Netherlands

buitenstverlaat.e: First attested as Verlaat (Het Buitenste) in 1899. Compound of buitenst (“outermost”) and verlaat (“small lift-lock”).

s.def: a village in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

bakkeveen.e: First attested as backenvene in 1232-1233. Calque of West Frisian Bakkefean, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Bakke (a personal name) and fene (“peat bog, marsh”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

beetsterzwaag.e: First attested as suagh in 1315. Calque of West Frisian Beetstersweach, derived in turn from a compound of the toponym Ald Beets and Old Frisian swâg (“pasture, land where cattle is kept”).

s.def: a hamlet in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

birstum.e: First attested as bersachon [read: bersathon] in 1315. Etymology uncertain. If the oldest attestation refers to the current settlement, a derivation from a compound of Old Frisian bêre (“barn, house”) or bêr (“manure”) and sâtha (“sod of ground”), sâth (“source, well”) or sate (“dwelling, domicil...

s.def: a hamlet in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Amersfoort, Utrecht, Netherlands

a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

bentemaden.e: Compound of bent (“moor grass, Molinia sp.”) and the plural form of made (“pasture”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

boekhorst.e: First attested as Bueckhorst in 1622. Compound of a dialectal form of beuk (“beech, Fagus sp.”) and horst (“overgrown elevated place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

bovenveld.e: Compound of boven (“upper, up-”) and veld (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

bûterheideveld.e: Calque of West Frisian Bûteheidefjild, a compound of bûter (“outer”), heide (“heath, heathland”) and fjild (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

buttinga.e: First attested as butinge and buttinga in the 17th century. Derived from West Frisian Buttinga (a surname).

s.def: a village in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

blesdijke.e: First attested as blesdic in 1350. Derived from a compound of a variant of either Middle Dutch blasse (“white spot, bald spot”) or a hydronym and dijk (“levee”).

s.def: a village in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

boijl.e: First attested as boilo in 1320. Compound of either a term cognate to Dutch Low Saxon Bühl (“small hill”), Middle Dutch Bode (a personal name) or boede (“shack, shed, small wooden building”) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

boekelte.e: First attested as Boekholt in 1718. Compound of a variant of beuk (“beech, Fagus sp.”) and holt (“rising deciduous forest”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

bilgaard.e: First attested as bolgaerd in 1440. Potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian *Bullinga (a surname) and werd (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”). A reading of the first element as bol (“rounded elevated place”) is less likely. Compare Wijtgaard.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

blitsaerd.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Blitsaerd, a compound of a term cognate to blinken (“flicker, shine”) and weer (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

bantega.e: First attested as Bantega in 1974. Compound of the toponym Bant (derived from bant (“area, inhabited area”)) and West Frisian ga (“village, village area”). Originally called Echtenpolder, a compound of the toponym Echten and polder (“polder”).

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

boornzwaag.e: First attested as bennigaswach in the 13th century. Calque of West Frisian Boarnsweach, a compound of Old Frisian Benninga (a surname) or Bonninga and swâg (“pasture, land where cattle is kept”).

s.def: a hamlet in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

ballingbuur.e: First attested as Balling Buir in 1718. Calque of West Frisian Ballingbuer, derived from a compound of Balling (a surname) and buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

bargebek.e: First attested as Bargebek in 1936. Borrowed from West Frisian De Bargebek, a compound of baarch (“pig”) and bek (“head”). Originally the name of a field shaped roughly like a pig's head.

s.def: a hamlet in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

boornzwaag over de wielen.e: Calque of West Frisian Boarnsweach oer de Wielen, derived from the toponym Boarnsweach, oer (“over”) and the plural form of wiel (“kolk lake”). The settlement is separated from the rest of Boornzwaag by a set of lakes.

s.def: a hamlet in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

bozum.e: First attested as bosingum in 1260. Borrowed from West Frisian Boazum, derived in turn from a contracted compound of Old Frisian Bose (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”). Compare Boesingheliede.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

britswerd.e: First attested as bretsenewarth in the second half of the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Britswert, derived from a compound of a term related to Old Frisian britsen (“break, shatter”) and werd (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

breezanddijk.e: First attested as Breezanddijk in 1973. Compound of the name of the construction island Breezand (derived from a compound of breed (“broad”) and zand (“sand”)) and dijk (“levee”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

burgwerd.e: First attested as borchwarth in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Burchwert, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian burg (“castle, levee”) and werd (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

baarderburen.e: First attested as Bairdt and Baerdt in the 16th century. Calque of West Frisian Baarderbuorren, derived in turn from a toponym Baard and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

baburen.e: First attested as Baeburen in 1529. Calque of West Frisian Beabuorren, a compound of an unclear first element and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”). The first element may be a personal name or baat (“benefit”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

barsum.e: First attested as Berswert in 1528. Borrowed from West Frisian Barsum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Beer (a personal name) and wert (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The second element was gradually replaced by hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

bernsterburen.e: First attested as Bernsera gued in 1506. Borrowed from West Frisian Barnsterbuorren, derived from a compound of an otherwise unknown toponym Beerns or Berns and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

bittens.e: Potentially attested as bottinge in the 10th century, attested with certainty as bittens in 1480. Borrowed from West Frisian Bittens, of unknown etymology. Potentially derived from a personal name.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

bloemkamp.e: First attested as Floridi Campi in 1200. Calque of West Frisian Blomkamp, a compound of blom (“flower”) and kamp (“field”). Originally the name of a Bernardine monastery.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

bonjeterp.e: First attested as Bonterp in 1543. Borrowed from West Frisian Bonjeterp, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Bonninga (a surname) and terp (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

bootland.e: First attested as Bootland in 1664. Calque of West Frisian Boatlân, a compound of Bote or Boate and lân (“land”).

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

baijum.e: First attested as baym in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Baaium, potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian Bado (a personal name) or Beie suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

bonkwerd.e: First attested as buurckwert in 1487. Borrowed from West Frisian Bonkwert, of uncertain etymology. Potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian *Bonninga (a surname) and wert (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

berlikum.e: First attested as berlichem in 1355. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Berilo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

beetgum.e: First attested as betingum around 1275. Potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian Bade (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

beetgumermolen.e: First attested as Beetgumer Meulen in 1622. Compound of the toponym Beetgum and molen (“mill”).

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

blessum.e: First attested as blesingum in 1250-1275. Borrowed from West Frisian Blessum, potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian Blesse (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

boksum.e: First attested as boxum in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Boksum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Bokke (a personal name) or Bokse and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

barrum.e: First attested as beerma buren in 1406. Borrowed from West Frisian Barrum, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Bera (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

ballum.e: First attested as jn balnera buren [read: balmera buren] in 1473. Borrowed from West Frisian Ballum, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Balle (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

baaiduinen.e:
Attested as Baaiduinen in 1899. Compound of baai (“bay”) and the plural form of duin (“dune”).

battenoord.e: First attested as battenoert in 1420 and 1421. Potentially derived from a compound of Middle Dutch Batto (a personal name) or batte (“quay, levee”) and ort (“place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Nissewaard, South Holland, Netherlands

beerenplaat.e: Attested as Beere Polder in 1866. Compound of the name of the Beere Polder and plaat (“sandbar visible at low tide”).

barendrechtse veer.e: Attested as Barendrechtsche Veer in 1839-1859. Compound of the toponym Barendrecht and veer (“ferry, crossing”).

bolnes.e: First attested as op bolnes in 1374. Compound of Middle Dutch bol (“small sandbar or ridge in a river which is visible at low tide”) and nes (“headland, spit”).

a hamlet in Hoeksche Waard, South Holland, Netherlands

blaaksedijk.e: Attested as BLAAKSCHE DIJK in 1840. Compound of the toponym De Blaak (a former hamlet) and dijk (“levee”).

bommelskous.e: First attested as bommersoirt in 1492. bentoniet.e: From English bentonite; equivalent to Benton + -iet.

bleskensgraaf.e: First attested as grauelant in 1255. Compound of a diminutive form of Middle Dutch Blasius (a personal name), and the hydronym Grave.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Hollands Kroon, North Holland, Netherlands

barsingerhorn.e: First attested as bersincshorne in 1289. Compound of an otherwise unknown toponym *Bersing (derived from Middle Dutch Barse (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-) and horn (“protruding bend, corner”).

s.def: a village in Hollands Kroon, North Holland, Netherlands

breezand.e: Attested as Breesant in 1665. Compound of breed (“broad”) and zand (“sand bar”). Named after a former sand bar.

s.def: a village in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

burgerbrug.e: Attested as Burgerbrug of Eenigenburgerbrug in 1840. Compound of the toponym Eenigenburg and brug (“bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

burgervlotbrug.e: Attested as Burger Vlotbrug in 1839-1859. Compound of the toponym Eenigenburg and vlotbrug (“retractable floating bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Den Helder, North Holland, Netherlands

blauwe keet.e: Attested as De blauwe Keet in 1839-1859. Derived from blauw (“blue”) and keet (“wooden shack”). Named after a former shack.

s.def: a city and municipality of Utrecht, Netherlands

bunschoten.e:
First attested as bonscoten in 1294. Compound of an unclear first element and the dative plural form of Middle Dutch schoet (“piece of land which juts upwards”). The first element has been linked to Proto-Germanic *bunjô (“elevated place, height”) and Old English bune (“reed”).
Compare Bonnen, Bu...

s.def: a hamlet in Bunschoten, Utrecht, Netherlands

bonte poort.e: First attested as Jan Rutgersengoet in 1608. Derived from bont (“motley”) and poort (“gate”). Named after a tollhouse-cum-inn named in turn after a nearby farmhouse. The element bont refers to the motley nature of the inn's customers.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Bunschoten, Utrecht, Netherlands

broerswetering.e: First attested as Broersweteringe in 1666. Compound of Broers (a surname) and wetering (“drainage channel”). Named after a nearby watercourse.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Bunschoten, Utrecht, Netherlands

bikkersvaart.e: First attested as Bickers Vaert in 1692. Compound of Bicker (a surname) and vaart (“canal”). Named after a nearby watercourse named in turn after Andries Bicker, merchant and mayor of Amsterdam.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Bunschoten, Utrecht, Netherlands

blokhuiswetering.e: Compound of Blokhuis (a surname) and wetering (“drainage channel”). Named after a nearby watercourse named in turn after a local family.

s.def: a village in De Bilt, Utrecht, Netherlands

bilthoven.e: First attested as Station in 1867. Compound of the toponym De Bilt and the plural form of hof (“farmstead, homestead”). The settlement's name was chosen by municipal vote in 1917 in order to avoid confusion with De Bilt.

s.def: a village in Zeist, Utrecht, Netherlands

bosch en duin.e: Attested as Bosch en Duin in 1913. Derived from bos (“forest”) and duin (“dune”). Named after the company which developed the settlement and after a set of dunes near De Bilt.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zeist, Utrecht, Netherlands

brugakker.e: First attested as enen heerwech geheyten brughacker in 1480. Likely a compound of a variant of Middle Dutch broec (“marsh, marshland”) and acker (“farmland”), referring in this case to a communal pasture.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zeist, Utrecht, Netherlands

blikkenburg.e: First attested as blikenberch in 1340. Compound of a term cognate to blijken (“shine, sparkle”) and Middle Dutch borch (“fortified settlement, castle”). Named after a former knightly manor.

s.def: a hamlet in Amersfoort, Utrecht, Netherlands

buurtsdijk.e: Attested as Buurtsdijk in 1868. Compound of the toponym *Buurt (derived from buurt (“hamlet, small settlement”)) and dijk (“levee”). Originally the name of a road.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amersfoort, Utrecht, Netherlands

bieshaar.e: First attested as de Bieshaar in 1830-1855. Compound of bies (“rush, Scirpus sp.”) and haar (“sandy ridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Leusden, Utrecht, Netherlands

bavoort.e: Potentially attested as bachevorth in 1006, attested with certainty as badenvorde in 1334. Compound of Old Dutch Bado (a personal name) and furda (“ford, crossing”).

bosveld.e: Compound of bos (“forest”) and veld (“field”). Named after a former farmhouse.
