bruine eng.e: First attested as Bruinen Ing in 1839-1859. Compound of bruin (“brown”) or De Bruin (a surname) and eng (“farmland next to a village”). The area may have been named after a local landowning family.

s.def: a hamlet in Utrechtse Heuvelrug, Utrecht, Netherlands

beerschoten.e:
Attested as Beerschoten in 1874. Potentially a compound of Middle Dutch bere (“manure, mud that looks like manure”) and schoet (“piece of land which juts upwards”).
Compare Baarschot.

s.def: a hamlet in Utrechtse Heuvelrug, Utrecht, Netherlands

breedeveen.e: First attested as Breedeveen in 1899. Compound of breed (“broad”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Utrechtse Heuvelrug, Utrecht, Netherlands

beukenstein.e: From beuk (“beech (Fagus sylvatica)”) + -en- + -stein (“manor”). Named after a former manor house.

batau.e: First attested in the 14th century. Derived from a variant of Middle Dutch betewe (“Betuwe, a natural region in Gelderland”). Named after a former castle.

a neighbourhood of Woerden, Utrecht, Netherlands

barwoutswaarder.e:
First attested as berenwerde in 1179. Compound of Middle Dutch Berewout (a personal name) and werde (“land next to water, riverine island, polder complex surrounded by water”). The second element was gradually reinterpreted to werder (“island, riverside land”).
The neighbourhood is named after th...

s.def: a hamlet in Woerden, Utrecht, Netherlands

bekenes.e: First attested as bekenesse in 1363. Compound of an unclear first element and Middle Dutch nesse (“headland, spit”). The first element may be Beke (a personal name) or a hydronym derived from beke (“brook, stream”), which would be a rarity for the polders west of Utrecht.

s.def: a hamlet in Woerden, Utrecht, Netherlands

breeveld.e: First attested as breclewelt [read: bredevelt] in 1174. Compound of Middle Dutch breet (“broad, wide”) and velt (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Woerden, Utrecht, Netherlands

breudijk.e: First attested as broedics velt and in den broedijc in 1280-1287. Compound of Middle Dutch bru (“sludge, gunk”) and dijc (“levee”). Compare Brouwhuis and Bruinisse.

baambrugge.e: First attested as bambrugge in 1394. Compound of Middle Dutch ban (“legal district, legal boundary”) and brugge (“bridge”). Compare Bambrugge.

baambrugse zuwe.e: Attested as VINKEVEENSCHE-ZUWE (DE)... wordt in de wandeling veelal de Baambrugsche-Zuwe genoemd in 1851. Derived from the toponym Baambrugge and zuwe (“sideways levee”).

botshol.e: First attested as Botsholemeer in 1575. Compound of an unclear first element and hol (“low-lying swampland”). The first element may be comparable to dialectal bodse (“swamp”) or bod (“clump of reeds standing at the edge of a piece of land”). Originally a hydronym.

s.def: a former municipality of Utrecht, Netherlands

breukelen-nijenrode.e: Derived from the toponyms Breukelen and Nijenrode.

breukelen-sint pieters.e: Derived from the toponym Breukelen and the name of Saint Peter. The area occupied by the municipality had been a legal district under the control of the Provosty of Saint Peter in Utrecht.

blauwkapel.e: First attested as Den blaucapel in 1575. Compound of blauw (“blue”) and kapel (“chapel”). Named after a chapel with a blue barrel vault.

s.def: former police force tasked with monitoring timber logging, rubber extraction, and the tribal population in the interior of Suriname
examples: In 1925 werd uit bezuinigingsoverwegingen de Dienst voor het Boswezen opgeheven en slechts een afgeslankte Bospolitie werd als onderdeel van het Departement van Landbouw gehandhaafd. De Bospolitie, die viel onder het oppertoezicht van de PG, was tevens belast met enkele speciale taken met betrekking tot de Bosnegers en de Indianen. Naast het uitvoeren van bestuursopdrachten moest zij controle uitoefenen op de verkoop van hout aan de handelaren en de verschuldigde retributie innen. Verder moest de Bospolitie toezicht houden op de situatie in de dorpen, het aankweken van voedingsgewassen stimuleren en in het algemeen inlichtingen inwinnen over de bevolking. Door gebrek aan menskracht was de Bospolitie nauwelijks in staat om al deze taken adequaat te vervullen.

De Cotinga's behooren tot de merkwaardigste vogels van Suriname en zijn van zeer uiteenloopende grootte. Een van de meest bekende, ofschoon men hem zelden te zien krijgt, maar des te meer hoort en over wiens klankvol geluid alle reizigers schrijven, is Lathria cinerea, (sur. Groenhartvogel, n.e. kwit-kwitti-jaba, naar het geluid, of Boesiskoutoe, Boschpolitieagent, arow. Olikako, kar. Paipei) een kleine, grijze vogel.

Op de kale rotsen broeden zowaar nachtzwaluwen, door hun schutkleur versmelten ze als het ware met de omgeving en je trapt bijna op ze voordat je ze ziet. Het pad naar beneden, glibberig geworden door een regenbui wordt begrensd door heerlijk ruikende orchideeën. In het oerwoud druppelt het nog wat na en de bospolitie is alweer overal te horen.

bospolitie.e: Literally, “forest police” or literally, “jungle police”, compound of bos (“forest, jungle”) + politie (“police”).

1895, Aug. P. van Groeningen, Een nest menschen, publ. by S.L. van Looy, page 57.
Buurvrouw dronk haar kopje leeg en zei: »Het is al te laat om aardappels te koken; ik zal maar wat blauwe bliksem klaar maken, dat is gauw klaar!«

blauwe bliksem.e: Literally, “blue lightning”. The sense for the mashed dish is after hete bliksem, with the peeling of cooking pears allegedly leading to blue (purple) hands.

buiteneiland.e: From buiten (“outside, out of”) + eiland (“island”). The island owes its name to being the furthest out into the IJmeer of all the islands of the IJburg archipelago, viewed from Amsterdam.

broodheer.e: From brood (“bread”) + heer (“gentleman, lord”). Compare German Brotherr.

s.def: deep-fried snacks such as are typically eaten as part of a bittergarnituur
examples: De bittergarnituur bestond uit bitterballen, kroketjes en ander bruin fruit.

s.def: semantic holism (thesis that the meaning of a word or statement can only be understood in the context of the whole)

betekenisholisme.e: From betekenis + holisme, calque of English semantic holism.

s.def: the city of Haarlem, viewed by Amsterdam yuppies as a place to move to in order to settle down and have children

baarlem.e: Blend of baren (“to give birth”) + Haarlem.

s.def: to regard something as financially viable
examples: Ik zie wel brood in je voorstel.

brood zien in.e: Literally, “to see bread in [something]”, bread being a metonymical reference to one's livelihood (one's daily bread).

examples: Dat we naar een kroeg zouden gaan stond buiten kijf, maar we wisten niet zeker welke.

buiten kijf staan.e: Literally, “to stand beyond disputation”; see kijven (“to argue, quarrel”).

s.def: salutation, courtesy, polite expression (customary phrase used to be polite)

beleefdheidsformule.e: From beleefdheid + -s- + formule. Perhaps a calque of French formule de politesse.

examples: Hij hield een bevlogen speech om zijn politieke idealen te promoten.

s.def: music with heavy bass, particularly genres such as techno, house or gabber

boemboemmuziek.e: From boem (“boom”) + boem (“boom”) + muziek (“music”), referring onomatopoeically to the sound of a bassline.

s.def: book department, books section (part of the store where books are sold)
a mercury; any plant of genus Mercurialis

bingelkruid.e: From German Bingelkraut, and see kruid.

s.def: Bosnian (person from Bosnia and Herzegovina or of Bosnian descent)

bofkont.e: Compound of boffen (“to be lucky”) + kont (“butt, bum”).

s.def: a mother who transports her children by cargo bike; in particular, a bourgeois urban liberal mother
not protected by a dike; situated between a dike and the water from which it protects

buitendijks.e: Earlier buitendijksch, from buiten (“outside”) + dijk (“dike”) + -s (“adjectival suffix”). A closely related adverb butendikes, butendijx is attested in Middle Dutch.

s.def: bridge-builder (person who establishes friendly relations between parties)

examples: De meeste ongevallen vinden binnenshuis plaats

s.def: binary (being in one of two mutually exclusive states)

binair.e: Borrowed from French binaire, from Latin binārius.

bleke morgenster.e: Literally, “pale goat's-beard”, referring to the color of the flowers, which are slightly pale compared to those of gele morgenster.

s.def: clipping of bruine kiekendief (“western marsh harrier”)
to come prepared, to be well-prepared

beslagen ten ijs komen.e: Literally, “to come to [the] ice shod”; a horse required special horseshoes in order to be able to effectively traverse bodies of ice. May also be interpreted as a reference to iceskating.

s.def: frying fat; fat used to fry in

bangebroek.e: From bang (“scared”) + -e- + broek (“pants, trousers”), in reference to someone soiling their pants when scared.

s.def: rebar mesh; metal mesh to reinforce concrete
to keep up (with something or someone)
examples: Ik kan dit tempo niet bijbenen.

bijbenen.e: From bij (“caught up, not lagging behind”) + been (“leg”) + -en.

s.def: to keep a cool head; to stay focused

policy officer (an expert employed by the government, a non-governmental organisation (such as hospitals, educational institutions and other large civil society organisations), or a (large) company, who helps to formulate and implement the policies or the strategy of their employer, often in a specific focus area)
examples: Draag jij graag bij aan een duurzaam watersysteem? En wil je het verschil maken voor onze klanten door ze te helpen met drinkwaterbesparing thuis en voor bedrijven?^([sic]) Dan zijn wij op zoek naar jou! Wat doet een beleidsadviseur Klantinteractie en klantenservice bij Vitens? (...) We zijn op zoek naar een nieuwe collega die met zijn/haar skills invulling kan geven aan de strategie op^([sic]) gedragsverandering m.b.t. drinkwater gebruik.^([sic – meaning drinkwatergebruik])

beleidsadviseur.e: Compound of beleid (“policy, strategy, course of action”) + -s- (genitival interfix) + adviseur (“adviser, counselor”).

s.def: to focus blindly, to pay too much attention to something and ignore bigger picture
examples: We moeten ons niet blindstaren op incidenten.

s.def: to leave one's area of expertise; to exceed one's purview; to be out of one's depth

examples: Zelfs laat men zwart brood door de ochtendnevel „bedauwen” en geeft het aan het vee als voer; het is dan, volgens dit bijgeloof, voor heel het verdere jaar tegen hekserij gevrijwaard.

Vurige gebeden zullen door Nederland's Katholieken in de week van 25 Mei tot 2 Juni eens extra veel en buitengewoon krachtige^([sic – meaning krachtig]) gestort worden en dan kan het niet anders of God zal ze bedauwen met Zijn zegen en genade en groot zal dan de oogst wezen, welke tot rijpen komt onder die in onze Indiën nog ronddolen in de duisternis van het heidendom of in het halflicht van den Islam.

betalingsherinnering.e: From betaling + -s- + herinnering. Compare German Zahlungserinnerung.

bijsmaak.e: From bij- + smaak. Cognate of Danish bismage, Swedish bismak, German Beigeschmack.

bromaat.e: From French bromate. Analyzed as broom + -aat.

s.def: basic need, one of basic needs
blood sugar level, blood sugar (amount of glucose in a person's blood)

bloedsuikerspiegel.e: From bloedsuiker + spiegel. Cognate with German Blutzuckerspiegel.

examples: En allebei geloofden ze de spreker die min of meer beweerde wat zij toch al dachten. Dat is het bevestigingsvooroordeel, legde die Vlaming vervolgens uit.

bevestigingsvooroordeel.e: From bevestiging + -s- + vooroordeel, a calque of English confirmation bias.

s.def: A dish consisting of mashed potatoes with flat beans and navy beans, often served with smoked sausage.

besteleend.e: Compound of bestellen (“to order; to have something delivered”) + eend (“Citroën 2CV”, literally “duck”).

s.def: 30 May 1787, the day that Orangist shipwrights rose up against the republican regent elite of Amsterdam and this uprising was bloodily repressed by the ruling elite
a day of reckoning, in particular one of bloody revenge or repression (if unrelated to the next sense)
the annual parliamentary debate concerning the audit report of the government budget, held on the third Wednesday in May
big day (day on which something significant or momentous happens), moment of truth

blauwe zegge.e: Literally, “blue sedge”, referring to the pale green (somewhat bluish, perhaps) foliage.

s.def: A type of fen meadow, specifically the phytoassociation Cirsio dissecti-Molinietum.

blauwgrasland.e: From blauw + grasland, referring to blauwe zegge, a typical plant species of this habitat. It does not refer to blauwgras (Sesleria albicans).

bosjavaan.e: Compound of bos (“forest, jungle”) + Javaan (“Javanese person”), due to the perceived similarity in physical appearance between Amerindians and Javanese.

s.def: freeing move (move that undoes restrictions on a player's space)
brother, bro, friend (specifically when referring to someone of Indo-Surinamese ethnicity)
brother, bro, friend (specifically when referring to someone of Maroon ethnicity)

baala.e: Borrowed from Aukan baala, from Sranan Tongo brada, from English brother.

s.def: traveller's joy, old man's beard (Clematis vitalba)

bosrank.e: Compound of bos (“forest”) + rank (“vine, winding plant, tendril”).

s.def: to run into a place, to enter by running
in fashion, in line with current tastes

A person who habitually sleeps on their stomach.

buikslaper.e: From buik (“stomach”) + slapen (“to sleep”) + -er, or buik + slaper.

backpacken.e: Borrowed from English backpack. Equivalent to backpack + -en.

examples: Het kompas werd bewaard in een kistje gemaakt van bukshout met fraai bewerkt koperbeslag.

s.def: a bead inserted permanently under the skin of the penis, supposedly in order to increase pleasure during vaginal or anal intercourse

boekverbranding.e: Compound of boek + verbranding. Compare German Bücherverbrennung.

s.def: to freeze, become stiff with cold
act of making known; announcement, disclosure
to bentsh (recite the Birkat Hamazon prayer)

bensjen.e: Borrowed from Yiddish בענטשן (bentshn). Doublet of benedijen.

benedijen.e: From Middle Dutch benediën, from Old Dutch benedīon, from Latin benedīcō. Doublet of bensjen.

examples: De expressionistische schilder bekladde het doek met wat verf.
De muren van het politiegebouw zijn beklad met anarchistische leuzen.

s.def: to one's knowledge, as far as one knows

blijde boodschap.e: Calque of Latin bona annūntiātiō, itself a calque from Ancient Greek εὐαγγέλιον (euangélion), or directly calqued from the Ancient Greek.

examples: Het filmpje buffert sneller als je een lagere videokwaliteit selecteert.

bufferen.e: Borrowed from English buffer. By surface analysis, buffer + -en.

s.def: second/third-person singular dependent-clause present indicative of binnendringen

begijn.e: From Middle Dutch begine, bagine, from Old French beguine.

s.def: to not be able to stand or tolerate, to have a bad experience
examples: Ik ga altijd bad op wiet.

bad gaan.e: From the informal copulative sense of gaan ("to feel some way about something"); bad is not used in Dutch (even in slang) besides this phrase and some borrowed phrases, like bad bitch. But note that this specific phrase is not a borrowing (although bad, which here functions as an adverb, is borro...

bodemvrijheid.e: Compound of bodem + vrijheid. Compare German Bodenfreiheit.

biomedisch.e: Borrowed from English biomedical. Equivalent to bio- + medisch.

back-uppen.e: Borrowed from English back up. Equivalent to back-up + -en.

examples: De berg steekt boven de wolken uit.
Ik moest schreeuwen om boven het geluid uit te komen.
Dat gevoel komt van binnenin.
Dit idee leeft binnenin zijn hart.

bepoederen.e: From be- -en + poeder (“powder”), alternatively analyzable as be- + poederen (“to powder”).

s.def: to be hypocritical, to not be innocent of something one accuses another of

boter op zijn hoofd hebben.e: Literally, “to have butter on his head”, a reference to the earlier proverb wie boter op het hoofd heeft, moet niet in de zon gaan lopen (“one who has butter on their head should not go walking in the sun”).

examples: Het duurde lang voordat de patiënt weer bij kennis was.

binnenlaten.e: Compound of binnen (“inside”) + laten (“to allow, let”).

s.def: a secret pertaining to one's profession
appointment (act of appointing someone to a position)
a diminutive of the male given name Bram, from Hebrew
a father who transports his children by cargo bike; in particular, a bourgeois urban liberal father
a parent who transports their children by cargo bike; in particular, a bourgeois urban liberal parent
minute of angle, arcminute (unit of angle)
examples: Coordinate term: boogseconde

boogminuut.e: Compound of boog (“arc, arch, bow”) + minuut (“minute”).

s.def: second of arc, arcsecond (unit of angle)
examples: Coordinate term: boogminuut

boogseconde.e: Compound of boog (“arc, arch, bow”) + seconde (“second”).

bouterse.e: From a name Bouter (equivalent to boud (“brave”) + heer (“army”); cognate with Old English Bealdhere, Old High German Baldheri or Baldhere and rendered in Medieval Latin as Baltharius, Baltherius) with the patronymic suffix -se. Compare Boutersem.

examples: Er is een probleem met de baanvastheid van de wagen.

s.def: a son of middle-class or bourgeois parents
examples: Coordinate term: burgerdochter

s.def: a daughter of middle-class or bourgeois parents
examples: Coordinate term: burgerzoon

begrijpelijkerwijs.e: A univerbation of begrijpelijker (“feminine genitive singular of begrijpelijk”) + wijs (“manner”), a genitive absolute phrase. Compare German begreiflicherweise. By surface analysis, begrijpelijk + -wijs.

examples: branderig gevoel

babbelguichje.e: The element babbel- is said to derive from a form related to French babiole and its Italian etymon (and not from babbelen). The second element is guich, guig (“mockery”).

s.def: A type of sausage or meatloaf similar to scrapple, made of pork scraps, flour and/or groats.

balkenbrij.e: The first element is likely not balk but rather balg, here with a specific meaning of "belly" (compare Middle Dutch gebalchte (“guts, innards”)); the second element is brij.

s.def: a marketing term for any bread that is presented as being somehow rustic or rural to impart an air of authenticity and craftsmanship, regardless of recipe or place of production
Bear Island (an island of Svalbard, Norway)

bereneiland.e: From beer (“bear”) + -en- + eiland (“island”). So-named by Willem Barents in 1596.

braambes.e: From Middle Dutch [Term?], from Old Dutch [Term?], from Frankish *brāmabasi (whence also Old French framboise, which yielded the doublet framboos), variant of Proto-West Germanic *brāmabaʀi. By surface analysis, braam (“bramble”) + bes (“berry”).

s.def: An inner line; a line that is placed on the inside of something.
A cart, typically with two walls, used within a hotel to move the luggage belonging to a guest between the parking lot or entrance and the room where the guest is staying, a bell cart.
examples: Zijn collega heeft onze koffers al uit de taxi gehaald. Binnen in het hotel staat al een bagagekar klaar, zo'n heuse sjieke die je altijd in films ziet.

s.def: the act or process of paving with clinkers

bolletjesvaren.e: From bolletje (“little ball”) + -s- + varen (“fern”); the sori look like little balls.

s.def: One’s own balcony, referred to as if it were a vacation destination; the place where one spends one’s staycation.

balkonië.e: From balkon (“balcony”) + -ië (“-ia, -y”). Compare German Balkonien meaning the same; the Dutch may be a calque of the German.

s.def: book club (commercial organization offering subscribers a selection of books)
curtain lecture (scolding given by a wife to her husband in bed)
examples: Te Hüningen, in Elzas-Lotharingen, kwam het sociaaldemocratische raadslid Enderlin dezer dagen na middernacht beschonken thuis. Zijne vrouw hield een bedsermoen en de man trok zich hare verwijten zoo sterk aan, dat hij een mes greep en zich daarmee in het hart stak.

Zondagnamiddag, zonk de heer van Heezebeeck, voorzitter van Beerschot, supporterskring, binst de rust, tijdens den match Beerschot—Football-club Mechelen plots ineen, en overleed tengevloge^([sic – meaning ten gevolge]) van hartaderbreuk

binst.e: From Middle Dutch bin + -s, with a paragogic -t.

bovenbedoeld.e: Univerbation of boven + bedoeld, past participle of bedoelen.

beekpunge.e: First attested in 1608 spelled beeckpunge, representing a calque of German Bachbunge / Bachpunge, where beek (“brook”) replaces German Bach; so named because the plant grows by brooks and ditches. The element -punge is an unadapted borrowing from German and does not exist outside this word in Dut...

s.def: first/second/third-person singular dependent-clause present indicative of binnenlaten
philadelphus, mock orange (Any of the genus Philadelphus of shrubs)

brileider.e: Compound of bril (“glasses”) + eider (“eider duck”), referring to the patterning around the eyes of the male of this species; compare also the English vernacular name spectacled eider.

boegeroep.e: Compound of boe (“boo”) + geroep (“clamoring, shouting, calling”).

s.def: leaf beetle (insect of the family Chrysomelidae)
a fault confessed is half redressed
subject to taxation; obliged to pay taxes; taxable
The state or quality of being subject to taxation; taxability.

examples: boventallig verklaard
[…] het pensioen waarvan zelfs een sober schamel man […] niet aan land kan leven of het moet zijn in een ballingsoord.

Na de knikkertijd was het veelal bat en bal tijd^([sic – meaning bat-en-baltijd]). Dan zag je de jongens op straat rondlopen gewapend met een tennisbal (of een bal gemaakt van balata) en een bat (vaak gemaakt van de tak van een kokospalm) op zoek naar tegenstanders, straat in straat uit. Het spel had veel weg van cricket met dit verschil dat er maar een enkele wicket werd gebruikt.
P. DE WILDE staat voor U klaar met de meest moderne machine voor het besproeien en benevelen van al Uw Landbouwgewassen
„[...] Uit een onderzoek is gebleken, dat de hele zaak zorgvuldig was voorbereid om propaganda te maken, om de krijgsgevangenenkwestie te Panmoendjon te benevelen”.

De zanger van de Noorse band met de charmante naam The Cumshots - bloot bovenlijf, veel tatoeages en erg beneveld - legde het optreden minutenlang stil om uit zijn hoofd complete verzen uit het bijbelboek Leviticus te citeren.

bolderik.e: From Middle Dutch bolric, probably derived from bol (“round”), referring to the shape of the seed capsule. For the suffix, compare wederik, dravik.

s.def: Jersey cudweed (Pseudognaphalium luteoalbum syn. Gnaphalium luteoalbum)

examples: Hij zag de struikrovers hun buit het rovershol binnenslepen.
Het grote containerschip liet zich door twee sleepboten binnenslepen.
Als we onze prijs 10 procent verlagen, gaan we deze opdracht binnenslepen.

blazoen.e: From Middle Dutch blasoen, from Old French blason.

s.def: logo (visual symbol or emblem that acts as a trademark or a means of identification of a company or organization.)
pack, equipment, gear; that which one has packed for travel, generally referring only to that which is carried on one's person, such as a backpack
examples: De soldaten marcheerden dag en nacht met zware bepakking.

bostonvaren.e: Compound of Boston + varen, calque of English Boston fern.

boerkini.e: Blend of boerka + bikini, calque of English burqini.

s.def: burqini ban, ban on wearing a burqini

examples: ‘Bekbedekkâh’ en kerauna eidisie van Ut Groen-Geile Boekie voor burgemeester Van Zanen

s.def: a kind of good female spirit
any mythological female creature, such as elves or witches

beeldwit.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch belewitte (“elf, good spirit”), from Old Dutch *biliwitti, from Proto-West Germanic *biliwiti, from a compound of Proto-Germanic *biliz (“kind, gentle”) + *witją (“wit”). Compare German Bilwis (“a kind of demon”) and Old English bilewit (“innocent, calm”).

s.def: Belgian Congo (a former Belgian colony in Central Africa, existing from 1908 to 1960, when it became the modern Democratic Republic of the Congo)
Australian brush-turkey, bush turkey, scrub turkey (Alectura lathami)
Any cat living in the mountains.

brugspin.e: Compound of brug (“bridge”) + spin (“spider”); the spider often weaves webs on bridges.

butoor.e: From Middle Dutch butoor, from Old French butor, from Vulgar Latin *butitaurus, from būtiō (“bittern”) + taurus (“bull”), because of its booming call; compare also Middle Dutch watervar (“bittern”, literally “water bull”).

s.def: partitive of belangrijker, the comparative degree of belangrijk
a slut, particularly one who can be seduced with free alcohol
to throw in the towel; to give up; to resign oneself to a situation

babbelkont.e: Compound of babbelen (“to babble”) + kont (“butt, ass”).

bietser.e: By surface analysis, bietsen (“to scrounge, bum”) + -er, but the verb is in fact formed from this noun, which derives (via earlier forms bietsjer and bietskommer, which are very sparsely attested) from English beachcomber.

beduusd.e: Originally the past participle of a verb beduzen, properly beduizen, a prefixed form of duizen.

bezaantje.e: From bezaan + -tje. The hydrozoan species is named after the sense "mizzensail" of bezaan, referring to its above-water "sail" which allows it to be propelled by the wind.

s.def: blue dragon (sea slug of species Glaucus atlanticus)

examples: We kwamen wat broodjes tekort, dus we moesten er snel een paar bijbakken.

beekprik.e: Compound of beek (“brook, rivulet”) + prik (“lamprey; that which stings”).

examples: Maar de duidelijke, snelle veroordeling die Polen nu bezigt, duidt wel op een nieuwe fase.

bezigen.e: From Middle Dutch besighen; bēsich in Middle Dutch had a meaning of "useful" which is lost in Modern Dutch, so the surface analysis of bezig + -en is somewhat unintuitive to modern speakers.

s.def: A motley troop, mishmash, large and varied collection.

bibelebontse berg.e: A reference to an old children's song about people living on a fictional mountain known as the Bibelebontse berg. The origin of Bibelebonts is unclear; some variants are known from the nineteenth century such as Biebelabonsch and Bibelebomsch.

s.def: The act of reading from the Bible.
spice mix used in Indonesian cooking

bloeden als een rund.e: Literally, “to bleed like a head of cattle”. Probably a reference to the slaughterhouse.

s.def: course (path that something or someone moves along)

beloop.e: From Middle Dutch beloop. By surface analysis, deverbal from belopen.

s.def: to enter, to step into a place

blij ei.e: Literally, “happy egg”; the expression does not reference anything in particular and owes its usage mostly to its rhyming quality and absurdity.

s.def: bottle used for washing the buttocks/genitals after defecating/urinating (in Indo-Dutch households)

berakken.e: Adapted borrowing of Malay berak (“to poop”) + -en.

betreurenswaard.e: From the gerund of betreuren (“to regret”) + -s- + waard.

examples: De bowlingschoen rook naar tenenkaas.

s.def: external end of the cervix; os

bacteriedodend.e: From bacterie + dodend (present participle of doden).

s.def: barre chord: a guitar chord played by pressing down multiple strings on the fretboard.
brown café, an old-fashioned Dutch café with dim lighting and a lot of dark brown wood
examples: Het bruine café ontstond begin 19e eeuw, toen meer en meer mensen als extra kostwinning hun voorkamer inrichtten als plaats waar mensen iets konden drinken.

s.def: A type of wine, previously used medicinally, containing various added ingredients such as herbs and spices.
drivable (capable of being driven on safely or successfully)
brain rot (media content that is deemed to hold little artistic value or negatively impact those who consume it)

breinrot.e: Calque of English brain rot. By surface analysis, brein + rot.

s.def: synonym of minister van Binnenlandse Zaken (“interior minister”)
examples: […] geflankeerd door binnenlandminister Diosdado Cabello (r.)[…]

binnenlandminister.e: Likely a calque of English interior minister and/or German Innenminister, equivalent to binnenland + minister.

s.def: the speed limit on a section of railway track
to bronze, to brown, to cause to become tan in colour
only used in op de bonnefooi

bijker.e: Formed in analogy to imker, using bij in place of imme.

blauwe monnikskap.e: Literally, “blue monkshood”, referring to the color of the flower; not all aconite flowers are blue.
